revision.text:
'''淫祀'''指不合適的[[祭祀]]或祭祀不在國家[[祀典]]當中的[[神祇|神明]]，即现代所谓“[[民间宗教]]”<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |title=民间信仰与官方意识 |accessdate=2020-03-29 |archive-date=2020-03-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200329052104/http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |dead-url=no }}</ref>。古代中國為了維護社會秩序，以“[[禮]]”作為維繫道德倫理之準則，希望人民能因為禮的教化而建立良好的道德觀，為了避免百姓道德倫理的價值觀發生錯亂，規定诸禁。這種觀念也見於[[漢字文化圈]]其他國家。
關於宗教信仰之部份則為禁止人民進行各種“淫祀”。《[[禮記]]·曲禮》謂：「非其所祭而祭之，名曰淫祀。淫祀無福。」“敦孝悌以重人倫，篤宗族以昭雍睦，和鄉黨以息爭訟，重農桑以足衣食，尚節儉以息財用，隆學校以端士習，黜異端以崇正學，講法律以儆愚頑，明禮讓以厚風俗，務本業以定民志，訓子弟以禁非為，息誣告以全善良，誡窩逃以免株連，完錢糧以省催科。”<ref>《聖祖實錄·康熙九年癸巳條》</ref>
清人[[孫希旦]]注稱：“非所祭而祭之，謂非所當祭之鬼而祭之也；淫，過也，或其神不在祀典，如[[宋襄公]]祭次睢之社，或越分而祭，如魯[[季氏]]之旅[[泰山]]，皆淫祀也。淫祀本以求福，不知淫昏之鬼不能福人，而非禮之祭，明神不歆也。”
== 内涵 == 所謂的淫祀包含了'''越份之祭'''與'''未列入祀典之祭'''兩種。中國神祇有三大部份，分為[[天神]]、[[地祇]]、[[人鬼]]<ref>《[[周禮]]·春官·大宗伯》：“掌建邦之天神、人鬼、地祇之禮。”</ref>，在此不論天神、地祇（及[[物精]]），只論人鬼之部份，按《禮記．祭法》載：“夫聖王之制祭祀也，法施於民則祀之，以死勤事則祀之，以勞定國則祀之，能禦大菑則祀之，能捍大患則祀之。”可知能為人所祭之人鬼，除了家家戶戶自己的祖先之外，就只能是有功於國家地方者方得受祠祀之享。
雖說有功之人得受享祀，但是“非其所祭而祭之”亦屬「淫祀」。以[[江南]]之地為例，過去江南曾有“三高祠”祀[[范蠡]]、[[張翰 (晉朝)|張翰]]、[[陸龜蒙]]，三忠祠祀[[伍員]]、[[張巡]]、[[岳飛]]，然士人們皆認為“三高祠”不應祠祀范蠡，因其曾進美女、寶器以惑吳王，為吳人之仇敵，“禮不祀非族，況可仇敵乎？”是以不得祀也。張巡、岳飛雖忠，但與吳地毫無瓜葛，亦不應祀，祭之則屬淫祀，“古之有功德於民者，廟食百世，豈無其所，而顧為愚夫愚婦之所假託哉。”<ref>《吳江縣志·祠廟》稱：“今於祠廟之志，不得不嚴，稍寬其途，則不肖者遂將緣志之例，已售其奸，而遇民遂因之以徵信，其風將不可止矣。古之有功德於民者，廟食百世，豈無其所，而顧為愚夫愚婦之所假託哉。”</ref>。
== 历史中的淫祀 == 與國家認定的正統神祇所相反的淫祀，其所受到的待遇則與正統神祇相反，歷來皆為士人所譏，甚而以武力所禁。例如[[西漢]][[平帝]]時有「班教化、禁淫祀、放鄭聲。」；[[東漢]][[第五倫]]「禁絕淫祀，人皆安之。」；東漢末[[曹操]]「禁斷淫祀，姦宄逃竄，郡界肅然。」；[[魏文帝]]詔「自今其敢設非禮之祭，巫祝之言，皆以執左道論，著于令。」，[[魏明帝]]詔「郡國山川不在祀典者，勿祠。」；[[晉武帝]]時詔「不在祀典，除之。」；北魏明帝「詔除淫祀，焚諸雜神。」；[[北周]][[武帝]]「禁諸淫祀，禮典所不載者，盡除之。」；[[南北朝|南朝]][[宋武帝]]「禁淫祀，除諸房廟。」。
由於「淫祀」之定義向為「官方」所訂之，是以此時被視之為正祀之神，在彼時又被認為是妖淫精鬼。六朝之前，[[佛教]]在中土多為士人認為「佛，外國之神，非諸華所應祠奉」之淫祀；[[蔣子文]]傳說於三國時期產生，最初是顯靈迫[[孫權]]為之立廟，[[東晉]]時被尊為晉守護神，[[南北朝|南朝]][[宋武帝]]時被視為淫祀而毀壞其廟宇，然而[[南朝宋]]又於孝武帝時復起蔣山祠，之後[[蔣子文]]信仰在南朝即當穩固，甚而被封為「帝」。然至宋時，其聲勢則為[[關羽]]所掩蓋，[[明代]]時其位則從「帝」被貶為「公」或「王」。[[項羽]]兵敗自刎死後被尊之為神，在漢代自然被視為淫祀，然[[陳武帝]]封其為「帝」，是重要的武神。至宋代後，地位亦被[[關羽]]取代。
* [[宋代]]以後，開始人格化的[[文昌帝君]] *: 原本乃[[文昌星]]，星宿神祇。宋代[[道教]]地方信仰興起，兼之政府開始大量科舉取仕，在[[四川]]，[[梓潼]]地方性的人鬼「[[張亞子]]」，被視為文昌星化身。也因此，究竟是地方神靈，還是文運之神，經過數百年的爭議，終至嘉慶六年（1801年）進入祀典，正式被認為是文運之神。由於該人鬼信仰源於[[梓潼]]，故又有梓潼帝君之稱號。
*[[明代]]的[[玄天上帝]] *: 此神祇源於遠古四帝、四靈、四象與星宿信仰之結合，[[道教]]成形之後，其位格亦屬道教大神。於宋代時成為人格神。其成為明代護國家神有兩種傳說，分別為曾顯神跡幫助[[明太祖]]與[[明成祖]]，故成為明代官方神祇，[[明鄭]]時期此神祇亦是臺灣最主要之信仰之一，後至清領時期因政治因素，被大力貶抑，以[[媽祖]]此信仰取代。
*[[清代]]的「[[關聖帝君]]」 *: 有關清代關羽信仰的論文所在多有，故不多談。此中「帝」乃因道教素有「帝君」之神號，故賜此「帝君」之號乃致其尊，非封爵，民間常以此字附會其地位與帝王齊平，乃以字害意之誤也。
*另有「[[媽祖|天后]]」（傳說因[[媽祖]]有功於[[施琅]]攻[[臺灣]]、[[澎湖]]，故為清廷晉封為天后）、「[[雙忠]]」（張巡、許遠）、等以及地方神祇如[[臺灣]]的「[[保生大帝]]」、「[[開漳聖王]]」、「[[清水祖師]]」、「[[青山王]]」、「[[三山國王]]」等。
== 参考文献 == {{Reflist}}
== 參見 == {{Wiktionary}} *[[淫祠]] *[[邪教]] *[[宗教迫害]] *[[民间宗教]] *[[被神化的人列表]]
[[Category:神]] [[Category:東亞民間信仰]]

'''淫祀'''指不合適的[[祭祀]]或祭祀不在國家[[祀典]]當中的[[神祇|神明]]，即现代所谓“[[民间宗教]]”<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |title=民间信仰与官方意识 |accessdate=2020-03-29 |archive-date=2020-03-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200329052104/http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |dead-url=no }}</ref>。古代中國為了維護社會秩序，以“[[禮]]”作為維繫道德倫理之準則，希望人民能因為禮的教化而建立良好的道德觀，為了避免百姓道德倫理的價值觀發生錯亂，規定了各種禁止之行為。這種觀念也見於[[漢字文化圈]]其他國家。
關於宗教信仰之部份則為禁止人民進行各種“淫祀”。《[[禮記]]·曲禮》謂：「非其所祭而祭之，名曰淫祀。淫祀無福。」“敦孝悌以重人倫，篤宗族以昭雍睦，和鄉黨以息爭訟，重農桑以足衣食，尚節儉以息財用，隆學校以端士習，黜異端以崇正學，講法律以儆愚頑，明禮讓以厚風俗，務本業以定民志，訓子弟以禁非為，息誣告以全善良，誡窩逃以免株連，完錢糧以省催科。”<ref>《聖祖實錄·康熙九年癸巳條》</ref>

revision.comment: Created page with "{{AfC submission|t||ts=20220918031628|u=Immanuelle|ns=118|demo=}} {{Short description|Problematic rituals in Chinese culture}} {{Expand Chinese}} Obscene sacrifice is a term used to refer to sacrifices or [[Jesa]] which are inappropriate and contrary to national norms in the [[East Asian cultural sphere]], such rituals would fall under [[Folk religion]]<ref>{{Cite web |title=民间信仰与官方意识 |url=http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |archive-url=https://..."
/* References */
/* Overview */
revision.text:
{{AfC submission|t||ts=20220918031628|u=Immanuelle|ns=118|demo=}}{{afc comment|1=Draft was deleted on wikipedia and preserved on everybodywiki, see [[every:Obscene sacrifice]] for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 13:36, 30 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Problematic rituals in Chinese culture}} {{Expand Chinese}}
Obscene sacrifice is a term used to refer to sacrifices or [[Jesa]] which are inappropriate and contrary to national norms in the [[East Asian cultural sphere]], such rituals would fall under [[Folk religion]]<ref>{{Cite web |title=民间信仰与官方意识 |url=http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200329052104/http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |archive-date=2020-03-29 |access-date=2020-03-29}}</ref>。
The [[Book of Rites]] condemns obscene sacrifice<ref>《聖祖實錄·康熙九年癸巳條》</ref>
==Overview== '''Improper Worship''' (淫祀) refers to the improper {{ill|Sacrificial Ritual|de|Opferzeremonie|ja|祭祀|zh|祭祀}} or the worship of {{ill|Deities|de|Gottheiten|ja|神祇|lt=Divine Beings|lt_ja=神明|lt_de=Gottheiten}} not included in the state‐sanctioned {{ill|Sacrificial Canon|de|Opferkanon|ja|祀典|zh|祀典}}, that is, what is nowadays known as “{{ill|Folk Religion|de|Volksreligion|ja|民間宗教|zh|民间宗教}}”.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |title=民间信仰与官方意识 |accessdate=2020-03-29 |archive-date=2020-03-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200329052104/http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |dead-url=no }}</ref> In ancient China, in order to maintain social order, the concept of “{{ill|Ritual|de|Ritus|ja|禮|zh|禮}}” was established as the standard for upholding moral ethics, with the hope that the people, through the education of ritual, would develop sound moral values. To prevent the corruption of the populace’s ethical standards, various prohibited practices were stipulated. This idea was also found in other countries of the {{ill|Sinosphere|de|Sinosphäre|ja|漢字文化圏|zh|漢字文化圈}}.
With respect to religious beliefs, all forms of “improper worship” were forbidden. The “Qu Li” section of the {{ill|Book of Rites|de|Buch der Riten|ja|礼記|zh|禮記}} states: “To worship that which is not rightfully one's own is called improper worship. Improper worship brings no blessing.” “By fostering filial piety and fraternal respect to uphold human relationships, by consolidating the clan to manifest harmony, by reconciling the local community to quell disputes, by emphasizing agriculture to secure sustenance, by valuing frugality to restrain extravagance, by promoting schools to cultivate proper conduct, by suppressing heterodox doctrines to elevate orthodox learning, by expounding the law to warn the foolish, by clarifying courtesy and yielding to enrich customs, by attending to one’s essential duties to stabilize the people’s resolve, by instructing the young to deter wrongdoing, by curbing false accusations to protect the virtuous, by admonishing collusion to prevent guilt by association, and by ensuring proper finance and provisions to mitigate undue penalties.”<ref>《聖祖實錄·康熙九年癸巳條》</ref>
The Qing scholar {{ill|Sun Xidan|de|Sun Xidan|ja|孫希旦|zh|孫希旦}} commented: “Worshiping that which is not one’s designated object—that is, worshiping ghosts that are not meant to be venerated; ‘improper’ implies transgression. For instance, when a deity not included in the canon is worshiped—as in the case of {{ill|Duke Xiang of Song|de|Herzog Xiang von Song|ja|宋襄公|zh|宋襄公}} offering sacrifices at the secondary Shui society, or when worship exceeds one’s due share, as with the Ji Clan’s pilgrimage to {{ill|Mount Tai|de|Berg Tai|ja|泰山|zh|泰山}}—these acts are all improper worship. Improper worship originally aimed at procuring blessings, yet it was not understood that a chaotically revered ghost cannot bestow blessings, and that a sacrifice performed in a manner contrary to ritual does not please the deities.”
== Connotations == The term “improper worship” encompasses both '''exceeding one’s due share in worship''' and '''worship not included in the sacrificial canon'''. Chinese deities are divided into three major categories: {{ill|Heavenly Deities|de|Himmlische Gottheiten|ja|天神|zh|天神}}, {{ill|Earthly Deities|de|Irdische Gottheiten|ja|地祇|zh|地祇}}, and {{ill|Human Ghosts|de|Menschliche Geister|ja|人鬼|zh|人鬼}}<ref>《{{ill|Zhou Li|de|Zhou-Li|ja|周禮|zh|周禮}}·Spring Officials·Grand Ancestor》: “Overseeing the rites of the heavenly deities, human ghosts, and earthly deities who establish the nation.”</ref>. Here, irrespective of whether one considers heavenly deities, earthly deities (or {{ill|Spirits of Things|de|Geister der Dinge|ja|物精|zh|物精}}), the focus is solely on human ghosts. According to the sacrificial regulations recorded in the {{ill|Book of Rites|de|Buch der Riten|ja|禮記|zh|禮記}}: “In the system of rites established by the sage-kings, those benefitting the people are venerated; those who die in dedicated service are honored; those who labor to secure the state are celebrated; those capable of repelling great calamities are revered; and those able to avert major misfortunes are worshiped.” Thus, apart from the ancestors venerated by every household, only individuals who have rendered meritorious service to the state or local community are entitled to be honored.
Although meritorious individuals are deserving of veneration, worshiping that which is not rightfully one’s due is also considered “improper worship”. For example, in the region of {{ill|Jiangnan|de|Jiangnan|ja|江南|zh|江南}}, there formerly existed the “Three High Temples” which honored {{ill|Fan Li|de|Fan Li|ja|范蠡|zh|范蠡}}, {{ill|Zhang Han (Jin Dynasty)|de|Zhang Han (Jin-Dynastie)|ja|張翰 (晉朝)|lt=Zhang Han|lt_ja=張翰|lt_de=Zhang Han}}, and {{ill|Lu Guimeng|de|Lu Guimeng|ja|陸龜蒙|zh|陸龜蒙}}, as well as the “Three Loyal Temples” which venerated {{ill|Wu Yuan|de|Wu Yuan|ja|伍員|zh|伍員}}, {{ill|Zhang Xun|de|Zhang Xun|ja|張巡|zh|張巡}}, and {{ill|Yue Fei|de|Yue Fei|ja|岳飛|zh|岳飛}}. However, scholars generally held that the “Three High Temples” should not venerate Fan Li, because he once offered beauties and precious objects to seduce the King of Wu—thus becoming an enemy of the people of Wu: “If the rites do not honor one’s own clan, how could one possibly venerate an enemy?” Therefore, he should not be honored. Although Zhang Xun and Yue Fei were loyal, having no ties with the Wu region they should not be venerated; to do so would constitute improper worship: “In ancient times, those who rendered meritorious service to the people were enshrined in temples for a hundred generations. Was it that they had no rightful place, and yet they were exploited by foolish men and women?”<ref>《{{ill|Gazetteer of Wujiang County: Temples and Shrines|de|Wujiang-County Gazetteer: Temples and Shrines|ja|吳江縣志·祠廟|zh|吳江縣志·祠廟}}》 states: “Now, in the records of temples and shrines, if one is forced to be strict and slightly relaxes the guidelines, then the unworthy will eventually exploit this precedent—selling their treachery, and when the people follow suit in their investigations, the trend will become unstoppable. In ancient times, those who rendered meritorious service to the people were enshrined in temples for a hundred generations. Was it that they had no rightful place, and yet they were exploited by foolish men and women?”</ref>
== Improper Worship in History == Improper worship—that which stands in opposition to the deities recognized by the state as orthodox—has consistently been ridiculed by scholars and, at times, forcibly suppressed. For example, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ping|de|Kaiser Ping|ja|平帝|zh|平帝}} of the {{ill|Western Han|de|Westliche Han-Dynastie|ja|西漢|zh|西漢}}, there was the decree “Ban the dissemination of teachings, prohibit improper worship, and permit the sounds of Zheng.” During the {{ill|Eastern Han|de|Östliche Han-Dynastie|ja|東漢|zh|東漢}}, {{ill|Diwulun|de|Diwulun|ja|第五倫|zh|第五倫}} decreed “Ban and eradicate improper worship, and the people will be at peace.” At the end of the Eastern Han, {{ill|Cao Cao|de|Cao Cao|ja|曹操|zh|曹操}} ordered “Ban and sever improper worship; illicit relations and the flight of offenders led to a solemnity along county boundaries.” Later, {{ill|Emperor Wen of Wei|de|Kaiser Wen von Wei|ja|魏文帝|zh|魏文帝}} issued an edict stating, “From now on, whoever dares to establish a rite not conforming to propriety, or whose shamanic words are uttered, shall be dealt with as following the heterodox path, as inscribed in the law.” Similarly, {{ill|Emperor Ming of Wei|de|Kaiser Ming von Wei|ja|魏明帝|zh|魏明帝}} decreed, “For those mountains and rivers in the counties not included in the sacrificial canon, do not venerate them.” During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Wu of Jin|de|Kaiser Wu von Jin|ja|晉武帝|zh|晉武帝}}, an edict stated “If it is not in the sacrificial canon, remove it.” Emperor Ming of the {{ill|Northern Wei|de|Northern Wei|ja|北魏|zh|北魏}} decreed “Ban improper worship, and burn all miscellaneous deities.” In the {{ill|Northern Zhou|de|Nördliche Zhou|ja|北周|zh|北周}}, {{ill|Emperor Wu|de|Kaiser Wu|ja|武帝|zh|武帝}} ordered “Ban all improper worship; expel anything not recorded in the liturgical canon.” In the period of the {{ill|Southern and Northern Dynasties|de|Süd- und Nord-Dynastien|ja|南北朝|lt=Southern Dynasty|lt_ja=南朝|lt_de=Süd-Dynastie}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Wu of Song|de|Kaiser Wu von Song|ja|宋武帝|zh|宋武帝}}, it was decreed “Ban improper worship; eliminate all local temples.”
Because the definition of “improper worship” was formulated by official standards, the deities thus venerated were deemed proper, yet at the same time were regarded as lewd, demonic, and perverse spirits. Prior to the Six Dynasties, {{ill|Buddhism|de|Buddhismus|ja|佛教|zh|佛教}} in China was largely considered by scholars to be “a foreign deity not worthy of v

{{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Draft was deleted on wikipedia and preserved on everybodywiki, see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 13:36, 30 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{AfC submission|t||ts=20220918031628|u=Immanuelle|ns=118|demo=}} {{Short description|Problematic rituals in Chinese culture}} {{Expand Chinese}}
Obscene sacrifice is a term used to refer to sacrifices or [[Jesa]] which are inappropriate and contrary to national norms in the [[East Asian cultural sphere]], such rituals would fall under [[Folk religion]]<ref>{{Cite web |title=民间信仰与官方意识 |url=http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200329052104/http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |archive-date=2020-03-29 |access-date=2020-03-29}}</ref>。
The [[Book of Rites]] condemns obscene sacrifice<ref>《聖祖實錄·康熙九年癸巳條》</ref>
==Overview== '''Improper Worship''' (淫祀) refers to the improper {{ill|Sacrificial Ritual|de|Opferzeremonie|ja|祭祀|zh|祭祀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacrificial Ritual|qq=}} or the worship of {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deities|de|Gottheiten|ja|神祇|lt=Divine Beings|lt_ja=神明|lt_de=Gottheiten|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Deities|qq=}} not included in the state‐sanctioned {{ill|Sacrificial Canon|de|Opferkanon|ja|祀典|zh|祀典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacrificial Canon|qq=}}, that is, what is nowadays known as “{{ill|Folk Religion|de|Volksreligion|ja|民間宗教|zh|民间宗教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Folk Religion|qq=}}”.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |title=民间信仰与官方意识 |accessdate=2020-03-29 |archive-date=2020-03-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200329052104/http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |dead-url=no }}</ref> In ancient China, in order to maintain social order, the concept of “{{ill|Ritual|de|Ritus|ja|禮|zh|禮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritual|qq=}}” was established as the standard for upholding moral ethics, with the hope that the people, through the education of ritual, would develop sound moral values. To prevent the corruption of the populace’s ethical standards, various prohibited practices were stipulated. This idea was also found in other countries of the [[:en:Sinosphere|Sinosphere]].
With respect to religious beliefs, all forms of “improper worship” were forbidden. The “Qu Li” section of the [[:en:Book of Rites|Book of Rites]] states: “To worship that which is not rightfully one's own is called improper worship. Improper worship brings no blessing.” “By fostering filial piety and fraternal respect to uphold human relationships, by consolidating the clan to manifest harmony, by reconciling the local community to quell disputes, by emphasizing agriculture to secure sustenance, by valuing frugality to restrain extravagance, by promoting schools to cultivate proper conduct, by suppressing heterodox doctrines to elevate orthodox learning, by expounding the law to warn the foolish, by clarifying courtesy and yielding to enrich customs, by attending to one’s essential duties to stabilize the people’s resolve, by instructing the young to deter wrongdoing, by curbing false accusations to protect the virtuous, by admonishing collusion to prevent guilt by association, and by ensuring proper finance and provisions to mitigate undue penalties.”<ref>《聖祖實錄·康熙九年癸巳條》</ref>
The Qing scholar {{ill|Sun Xidan|de|Sun Xidan|ja|孫希旦|zh|孫希旦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sun Xidan|qq=}} commented: “Worshiping that which is not one’s designated object—that is, worshiping ghosts that are not meant to be venerated; ‘improper’ implies transgression. For instance, when a deity not included in the canon is worshiped—as in the case of {{ill|Duke Xiang of Song|de|Herzog Xiang von Song|ja|宋襄公|zh|宋襄公|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Duke Xiang of Song|qq=}} offering sacrifices at the secondary Shui society, or when worship exceeds one’s due share, as with the Ji Clan’s pilgrimage to [[:en:Mount Tai|Mount Tai]]—these acts are all improper worship. Improper worship originally aimed at procuring blessings, yet it was not understood that a chaotically revered ghost cannot bestow blessings, and that a sacrifice performed in a manner contrary to ritual does not please the deities.”
== Connotations == The term “improper worship” encompasses both '''exceeding one’s due share in worship''' and '''worship not included in the sacrificial canon'''. Chinese deities are divided into three major categories: [[:en:Tenjin|Heavenly Deities]], {{ill|Earthly Deities|de|Irdische Gottheiten|ja|地祇|zh|地祇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Earthly Deities|qq=}}, and {{ill|Human Ghosts|de|Menschliche Geister|ja|人鬼|zh|人鬼|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Human Ghosts|qq=}}<ref>《{{ill|Zhou Li|de|Zhou-Li|ja|周禮|zh|周禮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhou Li|qq=}}·Spring Officials·Grand Ancestor》: “Overseeing the rites of the heavenly deities, human ghosts, and earthly deities who establish the nation.”</ref>. Here, irrespective of whether one considers heavenly deities, earthly deities (or {{ill|Spirits of Things|de|Geister der Dinge|ja|物精|zh|物精|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirits of Things|qq=}}), the focus is solely on human ghosts. According to the sacrificial regulations recorded in the [[:en:Book of Rites|Book of Rites]]: “In the system of rites established by the sage-kings, those benefitting the people are venerated; those who die in dedicated service are honored; those who labor to secure the state are celebrated; those capable of repelling great calamities are revered; and those able to avert major misfortunes are worshiped.” Thus, apart from the ancestors venerated by every household, only individuals who have rendered meritorious service to the state or local community are entitled to be honored.
Although meritorious individuals are deserving of veneration, worshiping that which is not rightfully one’s due is also considered “improper worship”. For example, in the region of [[:en:江南|Jiangnan]], there formerly existed the “Three High Temples” which honored [[:en:Fan Li|Fan Li]], {{ill|Zhang Han (Jin Dynasty)|de|Zhang Han (Jin-Dynastie)|ja|張翰 (晉朝)|lt=Zhang Han|lt_ja=張翰|lt_de=Zhang Han|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhang Han (Jin Dynasty)|qq=}}, and {{ill|Lu Guimeng|de|Lu Guimeng|ja|陸龜蒙|zh|陸龜蒙|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lu Guimeng|qq=}}, as well as the “Three Loyal Temples” which venerated {{ill|Wu Yuan|de|Wu Yuan|ja|伍員|zh|伍員|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wu Yuan|qq=}}, [[:en:Zhang Xun (Tang dynasty)|Zhang Xun]], and [[:en:Yue Fei|Yue Fei]]. However, scholars generally held that the “Three High Temples” should not venerate Fan Li, because he once offered beauties and precious objects to seduce the King of Wu—thus becoming an enemy of the people of Wu: “If the rites do not honor one’s own clan, how could one possibly venerate an enemy?” Therefore, he should not be honored. Although Zhang Xun and Yue Fei were loyal, having no ties with the Wu region they should not be venerated; to do so would constitute improper worship: “In ancient times, those who rendered meritorious service to the people were enshrined in temples for a hundred generations. Was it that they had no rightful place, and yet they were exploited by foolish men and women?”<ref>《{{ill|Gazetteer of Wujiang County: Temples and Shrines|de|Wujiang-County Gazetteer: Temples and Shrines|ja|吳江縣志·祠廟|zh|吳江縣志·祠廟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gazetteer of Wujiang County: Temples and Shrines|qq=}}》 states: “Now, in the records of temples and shrines, if one is forced to be strict and slightly relaxes the guidelines, then the unworthy will eventually exploit this precedent—selling their treachery, and when the people follow suit in their investigations, the trend will become unstoppable. In ancient times, those who rendered meritorious service to the people were enshrined in temples for a hundred generations. Was it that they had no rightful place, and yet they were exploited by foolish men and women?”</ref>
== Improper Worship in History == Improper worship—that which stands in opposition to the deities recognized by the state as orthodox—has consistently been ridiculed by scholars and, at times, forcibly suppressed. For example, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ping|de|Kaiser Ping|ja|平帝|zh|平帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ping|qq=}} of the {{ill|Western Han|de|Westliche Han-Dynastie|ja|西漢|zh|西漢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Western Han|qq=}}, there was the decree “Ban the dissemination of teachings, prohibit improper worship, and permit the sounds of Zheng.” During the {{ill|Eastern Han|de|Östliche Han-Dynastie|ja|東漢|zh|東漢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Han|qq=}}, {{ill|Diwulun|de|Diwulun|ja|第五倫|zh|第五倫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Diwulun|qq=}} decreed “Ban and eradicate improper worship, and the people will be at peace.” At the end of the Eastern Han, [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] ordered “Ban and sever improper worship; illicit relations and the flight of offenders led to a solemnity along county boundaries.” Later, {{ill|Emperor Wen of Wei|de|Kaiser Wen von Wei|ja|魏文帝|zh|魏文帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wen of Wei|qq=}} issued an edict stating, “From now on, whoever dares to establish a rite not conforming to propriety, or whose shamanic words are uttered, shall be dealt with as following the heterodox path, as inscribed in the law.” Similarly, {{ill|Emperor Ming of Wei|de|Kaiser Ming von Wei|ja|魏明帝|zh|魏明帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ming of Wei|qq=}} decreed, “For those mountains and rivers in the counties not included in the sacrificial canon, do not venerate them.” During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Wu of Jin|de|Kaiser Wu von Jin|ja|晉武帝|zh|晉武帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wu of Jin|qq=}}, an edict stated “If it is not in the sacrificial canon, remove it.” Emperor Ming of the [[:en:Northern Wei|Northern Wei]] decreed “Ban improper worship, and burn all miscellaneous deities.” In the [[:en:Northern Zhou|Northern Zhou]], [[:en:Emperor Wu|Emperor Wu]] ordered “Ban all improper worship; expel anything not recorded in the liturgical canon.” In the period of the {{ill|Southern and Northern Dynasties|de|Süd- und Nord-Dynastien|ja|南北朝|lt=Southern Dynasty|lt_ja=南朝|lt_de=Süd-Dynastie|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern and Northern Dynasties|qq=}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Wu of Song|de|Kaiser Wu von Song|ja|宋武帝|zh|宋武帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wu of Song|qq=}}, it was decreed “Ban improper worship; eliminate all local temples.”
Because the definition of “improper worship” was formulated by official standards, the deities thus venerated were deemed proper, yet at the same time were regarded as lewd, demonic, and perverse spirits. Prior to the Six Dynasties, [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] in China was largely considered by scholars to be “a foreign deity not worthy of v

{{AfC submission|t||ts=20220918031628|u=Immanuelle|ns=118|demo=}} {{Short description|Problematic rituals in Chinese culture}} {{Expand Chinese}}

To prevent the corruption of the populace’s ethical standards, various prohibited practices were stipulated. This idea was also found in other countries of the [[:en:Sinosphere|Sinosphere]].
With respect to religious beliefs, all forms of “improper worship” were forbidden. The “Qu Li” section of the [[:en:Book of Rites|Book of Rites]] states: “To worship that which is not rightfully one's own is called improper worship. Improper worship brings no blessing.” “By fostering filial piety and fraternal respect to uphold human relationships, by consolidating the clan to manifest harmony, by reconciling the local community to quell disputes, by emphasizing agriculture to secure sustenance, by valuing frugality to restrain extravagance, by promoting schools to cultivate proper conduct, by suppressing heterodox doctrines to elevate orthodox learning, by expounding the law to warn the foolish, by clarifying courtesy and yielding to enrich customs, by attending to one’s essential duties to stabilize the people’s resolve, by instructing the young to deter wrongdoing, by curbing false accusations to protect the virtuous, by admonishing collusion to prevent guilt by association, and by ensuring proper finance and provisions to mitigate undue penalties.”<ref>《聖祖實錄·康熙九年癸巳條》</ref>
The Qing scholar {{ill|Sun Xidan|de|Sun Xidan|ja|孫希旦|zh|孫希旦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sun Xidan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} commented: “Worshiping that which is not one’s designated object—that is, worshiping ghosts that are not meant to be venerated; ‘improper’ implies transgression. For instance, when a deity not included in the canon is worshiped—as in the case of {{ill|Duke Xiang of Song|de|Herzog Xiang von Song|ja|宋襄公|zh|宋襄公|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Duke Xiang of Song|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} offering sacrifices at the secondary Shui society, or when worship exceeds one’s due share, as with the Ji Clan’s pilgrimage to [[:en:Mount Tai|Mount Tai]]—these acts are all improper worship. Improper worship originally aimed at procuring blessings, yet it was not understood that a chaotically revered ghost cannot bestow blessings, and that a sacrifice performed in a manner contrary to ritual does not please the deities.”
== Connotations == The term “improper worship” encompasses both '''exceeding one’s due share in worship''' and '''worship not included in the sacrificial canon'''. Chinese deities are divided into three major categories: [[:en:Tenjin|Heavenly Deities]], {{ill|Earthly Deities|de|Irdische Gottheiten|ja|地祇|zh|地祇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Earthly Deities|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}}, and {{ill|Human Ghosts|de|Menschliche Geister|ja|人鬼|zh|人鬼|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Human Ghosts|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>《{{ill|Zhou Li|de|Zhou-Li|ja|周禮|zh|周禮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhou Li|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 周礼}}·Spring Officials·Grand Ancestor》: “Overseeing the rites of the heavenly deities, human ghosts, and earthly deities who establish the nation.”</ref>. Here, irrespective of whether one considers heavenly deities, earthly deities (or {{ill|Spirits of Things|de|Geister der Dinge|ja|物精|zh|物精|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirits of Things|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), the focus is solely on human ghosts. According to the sacrificial regulations recorded in the [[:en:Book of Rites|Book of Rites]]: “In the system of rites established by the sage-kings, those benefitting the people are venerated; those who die in dedicated service are honored; those who labor to secure the state are celebrated; those capable of repelling great calamities are revered; and those able to avert major misfortunes are worshiped.” Thus, apart from the ancestors venerated by every household, only individuals who have rendered meritorious service to the state or local community are entitled to be honored.
Although meritorious individuals are deserving of veneration, worshiping that which is not rightfully one’s due is also considered “improper worship”. For example, in the region of [[:en:江南|Jiangnan]], there formerly existed the “Three High Temples” which honored [[:en:Fan Li|Fan Li]], {{ill|Zhang Han (Jin Dynasty)|de|Zhang Han (Jin-Dynastie)|ja|張翰 (晉朝)|lt=Zhang Han|lt_ja=張翰|lt_de=Zhang Han|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhang Han (Jin Dynasty)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Lu Guimeng|de|Lu Guimeng|ja|陸龜蒙|zh|陸龜蒙|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lu Guimeng|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, as well as the “Three Loyal Temples” which venerated {{ill|Wu Yuan|de|Wu Yuan|ja|伍員|zh|伍員|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wu Yuan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伍子胥}}, [[:en:Zhang Xun (Tang dynasty)|Zhang Xun]], and [[:en:Yue Fei|Yue Fei]]. However, scholars generally held that the “Three High Temples” should not venerate Fan Li, because he once offered beauties and precious objects to seduce the King of Wu—thus becoming an enemy of the people of Wu: “If the rites do not honor one’s own clan, how could one possibly venerate an enemy?” Therefore, he should not be honored. Although Zhang Xun and Yue Fei were loyal, having no ties with the Wu region they should not be venerated; to do so would constitute improper worship: “In ancient times, those who rendered meritorious service to the people were enshrined in temples for a hundred generations. Was it that they had no rightful place, and yet they were exploited by foolish men and women?”<ref>《{{ill|Gazetteer of Wujiang County: Temples and Shrines|de|Wujiang-County Gazetteer: Temples and Shrines|ja|吳江縣志·祠廟|zh|吳江縣志·祠廟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gazetteer of Wujiang County: Temples and Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}》 states: “Now, in the records of temples and shrines, if one is forced to be strict and slightly relaxes the guidelines, then the unworthy will eventually exploit this precedent—selling their treachery, and when the people follow suit in their investigations, the trend will become unstoppable. In ancient times, those who rendered meritorious service to the people were enshrined in temples for a hundred generations. Was it that they had no rightful place, and yet they were exploited by foolish men and women?”</ref>
== Improper Worship in History == Improper worship—that which stands in opposition to the deities recognized by the state as orthodox—has consistently been ridiculed by scholars and, at times, forcibly suppressed. For example, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ping|de|Kaiser Ping|ja|平帝|zh|平帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ping|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平帝 (漢)}} of the {{ill|Western Han|de|Westliche Han-Dynastie|ja|西漢|zh|西漢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Western Han|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 前漢}}, there was the decree “Ban the dissemination of teachings, prohibit improper worship, and permit the sounds of Zheng.” During the {{ill|Eastern Han|de|Östliche Han-Dynastie|ja|東漢|zh|東漢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Han|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後漢}}, {{ill|Diwulun|de|Diwulun|ja|第五倫|zh|第五倫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Diwulun|qq=}} decreed “Ban and eradicate improper worship, and the people will be at peace.” At the end of the Eastern Han, [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] ordered “Ban and sever improper worship; illicit relations and the flight of offenders led to a solemnity along county boundaries.” Later, {{ill|Emperor Wen of Wei|de|Kaiser Wen von Wei|ja|魏文帝|zh|魏文帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wen of Wei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} issued an edict stating, “From now on, whoever dares to establish a rite not conforming to propriety, or whose shamanic words are uttered, shall be dealt with as following the heterodox path, as inscribed in the law.” Similarly, {{ill|Emperor Ming of Wei|de|Kaiser Ming von Wei|ja|魏明帝|zh|魏明帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ming of Wei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} decreed, “For those mountains and rivers in the counties not included in the sacrificial canon, do not venerate them.” During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Wu of Jin|de|Kaiser Wu von Jin|ja|晉武帝|zh|晉武帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wu of Jin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an edict stated “If it is not in the sacrificial canon, remove it.” Emperor Ming of the [[:en:Northern Wei|Northern Wei]] decreed “Ban improper worship, and burn all miscellaneous deities.” In the [[:en:Northern Zhou|Northern Zhou]], [[:en:Emperor Wu|Emperor Wu]] ordered “Ban all improper worship; expel anything not recorded in the liturgical canon.” In the period of the {{ill|Southern and Northern Dynasties|de|Süd- und Nord-Dynastien|ja|南北朝|lt=Southern Dynasty|lt_ja=南朝|lt_de=Süd-Dynastie|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern and Northern Dynasties|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 南北朝時代}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Wu of Song|de|Kaiser Wu von Song|ja|宋武帝|zh|宋武帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wu of Song|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 劉裕}}, it was decreed “Ban improper worship; eliminate all local temples.”
Because the definition of “improper worship” was formulated by official standards, the deities thus venerated were deemed proper, yet at the same time were regarded as lewd, demonic, and perverse spirits. Prior to the Six Dynasties, [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] in China was largely considered by scholars to be “a foreign deity not worthy of v

{{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Draft was deleted on wikipedia and preserved on everybodywiki, see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 13:36, 30 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{AfC submission|t||ts=20220918031628|u=Immanuelle|ns=118|demo=}} {{Short description|Problematic rituals in Chinese culture}} {{Expand Chinese}}
Obscene sacrifice is a term used to refer to sacrifices or [[Jesa]] which are inappropriate and contrary to national norms in the [[:en:East Asian cultural sphere|East Asian cultural sphere]], such rituals would fall under [[Folk religion]]<ref>{{Cite web |title=民间信仰与官方意识 |url=http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200329052104/http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |archive-date=2020-03-29 |access-date=2020-03-29}}</ref>。
The [[Book of Rites]] condemns obscene sacrifice<ref>《聖祖實錄·康熙九年癸巳條》</ref>
==Overview== '''Improper Worship''' (淫祀) refers to the improper {{ill|Sacrificial Ritual|de|Opferzeremonie|ja|祭祀|zh|祭祀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacrificial Ritual|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祭}} or the worship of {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deities|de|Gottheiten|ja|神祇|lt=Divine Beings|lt_ja=神明|lt_de=Gottheiten|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Deities|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}} not included in the state‐sanctioned {{ill|Sacrificial Canon|de|Opferkanon|ja|祀典|zh|祀典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacrificial Canon|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, that is, what is nowadays known as “{{ill|Folk Religion|de|Volksreligion|ja|民間宗教|zh|民间宗教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Folk Religion|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民間信仰}}”.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |title=民间信仰与官方意识 |accessdate=2020-03-29 |archive-date=2020-03-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200329052104/http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |dead-url=no }}</ref> In ancient China, in order to maintain social order, the concept of “{{ill|Ritual|de|Ritus|ja|禮|zh|禮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritual|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” was established as the standard for upholding moral ethics, with the hope that the people, through the education of ritual, would develop sound moral values. To prevent the corruption of the populace’s ethical standards, various prohibited practices were stipulated. This idea was also found in other countries of the [[:en:Sinosphere|Sinosphere]].
With respect to religious beliefs, all forms of “improper worship” were forbidden. The “Qu Li” section of the [[:en:Book of Rites|Book of Rites]] states: “To worship that which is not rightfully one's own is called improper worship. Improper worship brings no blessing.” “By fostering filial piety and fraternal respect to uphold human relationships, by consolidating the clan to manifest harmony, by reconciling the local community to quell disputes, by emphasizing agriculture to secure sustenance, by valuing frugality to restrain extravagance, by promoting schools to cultivate proper conduct, by suppressing heterodox doctrines to elevate orthodox learning, by expounding the law to warn the foolish, by clarifying courtesy and yielding to enrich customs, by attending to one’s essential duties to stabilize the people’s resolve, by instructing the young to deter wrongdoing, by curbing false accusations to protect the virtuous, by admonishing collusion to prevent guilt by association, and by ensuring proper finance and provisions to mitigate undue penalties.”<ref>《聖祖實錄·康熙九年癸巳條》</ref>
The Qing scholar {{ill|Sun Xidan|de|Sun Xidan|ja|孫希旦|zh|孫希旦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sun Xidan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} commented: “Worshiping that which is not one’s designated object—that is, worshiping ghosts that are not meant to be venerated; ‘improper’ implies transgression. For instance, when a deity not included in the canon is worshiped—as in the case of {{ill|Duke Xiang of Song|de|Herzog Xiang von Song|ja|宋襄公|zh|宋襄公|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Duke Xiang of Song|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} offering sacrifices at the secondary Shui society, or when worship exceeds one’s due share, as with the Ji Clan’s pilgrimage to [[:en:Mount Tai|Mount Tai]]—these acts are all improper worship. Improper worship originally aimed at procuring blessings, yet it was not understood that a chaotically revered ghost cannot bestow blessings, and that a sacrifice performed in a manner contrary to ritual does not please the deities.”
== Connotations == The term “improper worship” encompasses both '''exceeding one’s due share in worship''' and '''worship not included in the sacrificial canon'''. Chinese deities are divided into three major categories: [[:en:Tenjin|Heavenly Deities]], {{ill|Earthly Deities|de|Irdische Gottheiten|ja|地祇|zh|地祇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Earthly Deities|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}}, and {{ill|Human Ghosts|de|Menschliche Geister|ja|人鬼|zh|人鬼|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Human Ghosts|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>《{{ill|Zhou Li|de|Zhou-Li|ja|周禮|zh|周禮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhou Li|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 周礼}}·Spring Officials·Grand Ancestor》: “Overseeing the rites of the heavenly deities, human ghosts, and earthly deities who establish the nation.”</ref>. Here, irrespective of whether one considers heavenly deities, earthly deities (or {{ill|Spirits of Things|de|Geister der Dinge|ja|物精|zh|物精|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirits of Things|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), the focus is solely on human ghosts. According to the sacrificial regulations recorded in the [[:en:Book of Rites|Book of Rites]]: “In the system of rites established by the sage-kings, those benefitting the people are venerated; those who die in dedicated service are honored; those who labor to secure the state are celebrated; those capable of repelling great calamities are revered; and those able to avert major misfortunes are worshiped.” Thus, apart from the ancestors venerated by every household, only individuals who have rendered meritorious service to the state or local community are entitled to be honored.
Although meritorious individuals are deserving of veneration, worshiping that which is not rightfully one’s due is also considered “improper worship”. For example, in the region of [[:en:江南|Jiangnan]], there formerly existed the “Three High Temples” which honored [[:en:Fan Li|Fan Li]], {{ill|Zhang Han (Jin Dynasty)|de|Zhang Han (Jin-Dynastie)|ja|張翰 (晉朝)|lt=Zhang Han|lt_ja=張翰|lt_de=Zhang Han|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhang Han (Jin Dynasty)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Lu Guimeng|de|Lu Guimeng|ja|陸龜蒙|zh|陸龜蒙|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lu Guimeng|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, as well as the “Three Loyal Temples” which venerated {{ill|Wu Yuan|de|Wu Yuan|ja|伍員|zh|伍員|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wu Yuan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伍子胥}}, [[:en:Zhang Xun (Tang dynasty)|Zhang Xun]], and [[:en:Yue Fei|Yue Fei]]. However, scholars generally held that the “Three High Temples” should not venerate Fan Li, because he once offered beauties and precious objects to seduce the King of Wu—thus becoming an enemy of the people of Wu: “If the rites do not honor one’s own clan, how could one possibly venerate an enemy?” Therefore, he should not be honored. Although Zhang Xun and Yue Fei were loyal, having no ties with the Wu region they should not be venerated; to do so would constitute improper worship: “In ancient times, those who rendered meritorious service to the people were enshrined in temples for a hundred generations. Was it that they had no rightful place, and yet they were exploited by foolish men and women?”<ref>《{{ill|Gazetteer of Wujiang County: Temples and Shrines|de|Wujiang-County Gazetteer: Temples and Shrines|ja|吳江縣志·祠廟|zh|吳江縣志·祠廟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gazetteer of Wujiang County: Temples and Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}》 states: “Now, in the records of temples and shrines, if one is forced to be strict and slightly relaxes the guidelines, then the unworthy will eventually exploit this precedent—selling their treachery, and when the people follow suit in their investigations, the trend will become unstoppable. In ancient times, those who rendered meritorious service to the people were enshrined in temples for a hundred generations. Was it that they had no rightful place, and yet they were exploited by foolish men and women?”</ref>
== Improper Worship in History == Improper worship—that which stands in opposition to the deities recognized by the state as orthodox—has consistently been ridiculed by scholars and, at times, forcibly suppressed. For example, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ping|de|Kaiser Ping|ja|平帝|zh|平帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ping|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平帝 (漢)}} of the {{ill|Western Han|de|Westliche Han-Dynastie|ja|西漢|zh|西漢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Western Han|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 前漢}}, there was the decree “Ban the dissemination of teachings, prohibit improper worship, and permit the sounds of Zheng.” During the {{ill|Eastern Han|de|Östliche Han-Dynastie|ja|東漢|zh|東漢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Han|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後漢}}, {{ill|Diwulun|de|Diwulun|ja|第五倫|zh|第五倫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Diwulun|qq=}} decreed “Ban and eradicate improper worship, and the people will be at peace.” At the end of the Eastern Han, [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] ordered “Ban and sever improper worship; illicit relations and the flight of offenders led to a solemnity along county boundaries.” Later, {{ill|Emperor Wen of Wei|de|Kaiser Wen von Wei|ja|魏文帝|zh|魏文帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wen of Wei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} issued an edict stating, “From now on, whoever dares to establish a rite not conforming to propriety, or whose shamanic words are uttered, shall be dealt with as following the heterodox path, as inscribed in the law.” Similarly, {{ill|Emperor Ming of Wei|de|Kaiser Ming von Wei|ja|魏明帝|zh|魏明帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ming of Wei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} decreed, “For those mountains and rivers in the counties not included in the sacrificial canon, do not venerate them.” During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Wu of Jin|de|Kaiser Wu von Jin|ja|晉武帝|zh|晉武帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wu of Jin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an edict stated “If it is not in the sacrificial canon, remove it.” Emperor Ming of the [[:en:Northern Wei|Northern Wei]] decreed “Ban improper worship, and burn all miscellaneous deities.” In the [[:en:Northern Zhou|Northern Zhou]], [[:en:Emperor Wu|Emperor Wu]] ordered “Ban all improper worship; expel anything not recorded in the liturgical canon.” In the period of the {{ill|Southern and Northern Dynasties|de|Süd- und Nord-Dynastien|ja|南北朝|lt=Southern Dynasty|lt_ja=南朝|lt_de=Süd-Dynastie|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern and Northern Dynasties|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 南北朝時代}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Wu of Song|de|Kaiser Wu von Song|ja|宋武帝|zh|宋武帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wu of Song|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 劉裕}}, it was decreed “Ban improper worship; eliminate all local temples.”
Because the definition of “improper worship” was formulated by official standards, the deities thus venerated were deemed proper, yet at the same time were regarded as lewd, demonic, and perverse spirits. Obscene sacrifice is a term used to refer to sacrifices or [[Jesa]] which are inappropriate and contrary to national norms in the [[:en:East Asian cultural sphere|East Asian cultural sphere]], such rituals would fall under [[:en:Folk Religion|Folk Religion]]<ref>{{Cite web |title=民间信仰与官方意识 |url=http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200329052104/http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |archive-date=2020-03-29 |access-date=2020-03-29}}</ref>。
The [[Book of Rites]] condemns obscene sacrifice<ref>《聖祖實錄·康熙九年癸巳條》</ref>
==Overview== '''Improper Worship''' (淫祀) refers to the improper {{ill|Sacrificial Ritual|de|Opferzeremonie|ja|祭祀|zh|祭祀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacrificial Ritual|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祭}} or the worship of {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deities|de|Gottheiten|ja|神祇|lt=Divine Beings|lt_ja=神明|lt_de=Gottheiten|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Deities|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}} not included in the state‐sanctioned {{ill|Sacrificial Canon|de|Opferkanon|ja|祀典|zh|祀典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacrificial Canon|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, that is, what is nowadays known as “{{ill|Folk Religion|de|Volksreligion|ja|民間宗教|zh|民间宗教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Folk Religion|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民間信仰}}”.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |title=民间信仰与官方意识 |accessdate=2020-03-29 |archive-date=2020-03-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200329052104/http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |dead-url=no }}</ref> In ancient China, in order to maintain social order, the concept of “{{ill|Ritual|de|Ritus|ja|禮|zh|禮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritual|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” was established as the standard for upholding moral ethics, with the hope that the people, through the education of ritual, would develop sound moral values. To prevent the corruption of the populace’s ethical standards, various prohibited practices were stipulated. This idea was also found in other countries of the [[:en:Sinosphere|Sinosphere]].
With respect to religious beliefs, all forms of “improper worship” were forbidden. The “Qu Li” section of the [[:en:Book of Rites|Book of Rites]] states: “To worship that which is not rightfully one's own is called improper worship. Improper worship brings no blessing.” “By fostering filial piety and fraternal respect to uphold human relationships, by consolidating the clan to manifest harmony, by reconciling the local community to quell disputes, by emphasizing agriculture to secure sustenance, by valuing frugality to restrain extravagance, by promoting schools to cultivate proper conduct, by suppressing heterodox doctrines to elevate orthodox learning, by expounding the law to warn the foolish, by clarifying courtesy and yielding to enrich customs, by attending to one’s essential duties to stabilize the people’s resolve, by instructing the young to deter wrongdoing, by curbing false accusations to protect the virtuous, by admonishing collusion to prevent guilt by association, and by ensuring proper finance and provisions to mitigate undue penalties.”<ref>《聖祖實錄·康熙九年癸巳條》</ref>
The Qing scholar {{ill|Sun Xidan|de|Sun Xidan|ja|孫希旦|zh|孫希旦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sun Xidan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} commented: “Worshiping that which is not one’s designated object—that is, worshiping ghosts that are not meant to be venerated; ‘improper’ implies transgression. For instance, when a deity not included in the canon is worshiped—as in the case of {{ill|Duke Xiang of Song|de|Herzog Xiang von Song|ja|宋襄公|zh|宋襄公|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Duke Xiang of Song|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} offering sacrifices at the secondary Shui society, or when worship exceeds one’s due share, as with the Ji Clan’s pilgrimage to [[:en:Mount Tai|Mount Tai]]—these acts are all improper worship. Improper worship originally aimed at procuring blessings, yet it was not understood that a chaotically revered ghost cannot bestow blessings, and that a sacrifice performed in a manner contrary to ritual does not please the deities.”
== Connotations == The term “improper worship” encompasses both '''exceeding one’s due share in worship''' and '''worship not included in the sacrificial canon'''. Chinese deities are divided into three major categories: [[:en:Tenjin|Heavenly Deities]], {{ill|Earthly Deities|de|Irdische Gottheiten|ja|地祇|zh|地祇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Earthly Deities|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}}, and {{ill|Human Ghosts|de|Menschliche Geister|ja|人鬼|zh|人鬼|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Human Ghosts|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>《{{ill|Zhou Li|de|Zhou-Li|ja|周禮|zh|周禮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhou Li|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 周礼}}·Spring Officials·Grand Ancestor》: “Overseeing the rites of the heavenly deities, human ghosts, and earthly deities who establish the nation.”</ref>. Here, irrespective of whether one considers heavenly deities, earthly deities (or {{ill|Spirits of Things|de|Geister der Dinge|ja|物精|zh|物精|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirits of Things|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), the focus is solely on human ghosts. According to the sacrificial regulations recorded in the [[:en:Book of Rites|Book of Rites]]: “In the system of rites established by the sage-kings, those benefitting the people are venerated; those who die in dedicated service are honored; those who labor to secure the state are celebrated; those capable of repelling great calamities are revered; and those able to avert major misfortunes are worshiped.” Thus, apart from the ancestors venerated by every household, only individuals who have rendered meritorious service to the state or local community are entitled to be honored.
Although meritorious individuals are deserving of veneration, worshiping that which is not rightfully one’s due is also considered “improper worship”. For example, in the region of [[:en:江南|Jiangnan]], there formerly existed the “Three High Temples” which honored [[:en:Fan Li|Fan Li]], {{ill|Zhang Han (Jin Dynasty)|de|Zhang Han (Jin-Dynastie)|ja|張翰 (晉朝)|lt=Zhang Han|lt_ja=張翰|lt_de=Zhang Han|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhang Han (Jin Dynasty)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Lu Guimeng|de|Lu Guimeng|ja|陸龜蒙|zh|陸龜蒙|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lu Guimeng|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, as well as the “Three Loyal Temples” which venerated {{ill|Wu Yuan|de|Wu Yuan|ja|伍員|zh|伍員|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wu Yuan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伍子胥}}, [[:en:Zhang Xun (Tang dynasty)|Zhang Xun]], and [[:en:Yue Fei|Yue Fei]]. However, scholars generally held that the “Three High Temples” should not venerate Fan Li, because he once offered beauties and precious objects to seduce the King of Wu—thus becoming an enemy of the people of Wu: “If the rites do not honor one’s own clan, how could one possibly venerate an enemy?” Therefore, he should not be honored. Although Zhang Xun and Yue Fei were loyal, having no ties with the Wu region they should not be venerated; to do so would constitute improper worship: “In ancient times, those who rendered meritorious service to the people were enshrined in temples for a hundred generations. Was it that they had no rightful place, and yet they were exploited by foolish men and women?”<ref>《{{ill|Gazetteer of Wujiang County: Temples and Shrines|de|Wujiang-County Gazetteer: Temples and Shrines|ja|吳江縣志·祠廟|zh|吳江縣志·祠廟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gazetteer of Wujiang County: Temples and Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}》 states: “Now, in the records of temples and shrines, if one is forced to be strict and slightly relaxes the guidelines, then the unworthy will eventually exploit this precedent—selling their treachery, and when the people follow suit in their investigations, the trend will become unstoppable. In ancient times, those who rendered meritorious service to the people were enshrined in temples for a hundred generations. Was it that they had no rightful place, and yet they were exploited by foolish men and women?”</ref>
== Improper Worship in History == Improper worship—that which stands in opposition to the deities recognized by the state as orthodox—has consistently been ridiculed by scholars and, at times, forcibly suppressed. For example, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ping|de|Kaiser Ping|ja|平帝|zh|平帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ping|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平帝 (漢)}} of the {{ill|Western Han|de|Westliche Han-Dynastie|ja|西漢|zh|西漢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Western Han|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 前漢}}, there was the decree “Ban the dissemination of teachings, prohibit improper worship, and permit the sounds of Zheng.” During the {{ill|Eastern Han|de|Östliche Han-Dynastie|ja|東漢|zh|東漢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Han|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後漢}}, {{ill|Diwulun|de|Diwulun|ja|第五倫|zh|第五倫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Diwulun|qq=}} decreed “Ban and eradicate improper worship, and the people will be at peace.” At the end of the Eastern Han, [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] ordered “Ban and sever improper worship; illicit relations and the flight of offenders led to a solemnity along county boundaries.” Later, {{ill|Emperor Wen of Wei|de|Kaiser Wen von Wei|ja|魏文帝|zh|魏文帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wen of Wei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} issued an edict stating, “From now on, whoever dares to establish a rite not conforming to propriety, or whose shamanic words are uttered, shall be dealt with as following the heterodox path, as inscribed in the law.” Similarly, {{ill|Emperor Ming of Wei|de|Kaiser Ming von Wei|ja|魏明帝|zh|魏明帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ming of Wei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} decreed, “For those mountains and rivers in the counties not included in the sacrificial canon, do not venerate them.” During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Wu of Jin|de|Kaiser Wu von Jin|ja|晉武帝|zh|晉武帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wu of Jin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an edict stated “If it is not in the sacrificial canon, remove it.” Emperor Ming of the [[:en:Northern Wei|Northern Wei]] decreed “Ban improper worship, and burn all miscellaneous deities.” In the [[:en:Northern Zhou|Northern Zhou]], [[:en:Emperor Wu|Emperor Wu]] ordered “Ban all improper worship; expel anything not recorded in the liturgical canon.” In the period of the {{ill|Southern and Northern Dynasties|de|Süd- und Nord-Dynastien|ja|南北朝|lt=Southern Dynasty|lt_ja=南朝|lt_de=Süd-Dynastie|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern and Northern Dynasties|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 南北朝時代}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Wu of Song|de|Kaiser Wu von Song|ja|宋武帝|zh|宋武帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wu of Song|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 劉裕}}, it was decreed “Ban improper worship; eliminate all local temples.”
Because the definition of “improper worship” was formulated by official standards, the deities thus venerated were deemed proper, yet at the same time were regarded as lewd, demonic, and perverse spirits. Prior to the Six Dynasties, [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] in China was largely considered by scholars to be “a foreign deity not worthy of v

The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Draft was deleted on wikipedia and preserved on everybodywiki, see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 13:36, 30 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{AfC submission|t||ts=20220918031628|u=Immanuelle|ns=118|demo=}} {{Short description|Problematic rituals in Chinese culture}} {{Expand Chinese}}
Obscene sacrifice is a term used to refer to sacrifices or [[Jesa]] which are inappropriate and contrary to national norms in the [[:en:East Asian cultural sphere|East Asian cultural sphere]], such rituals would fall under [[:en:Folk Religion|Folk Religion]]<ref>{{Cite web |title=民间信仰与官方意识 |url=http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200329052104/http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |archive-date=2020-03-29 |access-date=2020-03-29}}</ref>。
The [[:en:Book of Rites|Book of Rites]] condemns obscene sacrifice<ref>《聖祖實錄·康熙九年癸巳條》</ref>
==Overview== '''Improper Worship''' (淫祀) refers to the improper {{ill|Sacrificial Ritual|de|Opferzeremonie|ja|祭祀|zh|祭祀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacrificial Ritual|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祭}} or the worship of {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deities|de|Gottheiten|ja|神祇|lt=Divine Beings|lt_ja=神明|lt_de=Gottheiten|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Deities|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}} not included in the state‐sanctioned {{ill|Sacrificial Canon|de|Opferkanon|ja|祀典|zh|祀典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacrificial Canon|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, that is, what is nowadays known as “{{ill|Folk Religion|de|Volksreligion|ja|民間宗教|zh|民间宗教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Folk Religion|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民間信仰}}”.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |title=民间信仰与官方意识 |accessdate=2020-03-29 |archive-date=2020-03-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200329052104/http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |dead-url=no }}</ref> In ancient China, in order to maintain social order, the concept of “{{ill|Ritual|de|Ritus|ja|禮|zh|禮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritual|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” was established as the standard for upholding moral ethics, with the hope that the people, through the education of ritual, would develop sound moral values. To prevent the corruption of the populace’s ethical standards, various prohibited practices were stipulated. This idea was also found in other countries of the [[:en:Sinosphere|Sinosphere]].
With respect to religious beliefs, all forms of “improper worship” were forbidden. The “Qu Li” section of the [[:en:Book of Rites|Book of Rites]] states: “To worship that which is not rightfully one's own is called improper worship. Improper worship brings no blessing.” “By fostering filial piety and fraternal respect to uphold human relationships, by consolidating the clan to manifest harmony, by reconciling the local community to quell disputes, by emphasizing agriculture to secure sustenance, by valuing frugality to restrain extravagance, by promoting schools to cultivate proper conduct, by suppressing heterodox doctrines to elevate orthodox learning, by expounding the law to warn the foolish, by clarifying courtesy and yielding to enrich customs, by attending to one’s essential duties to stabilize the people’s resolve, by instructing the young to deter wrongdoing, by curbing false accusations to protect the virtuous, by admonishing collusion to prevent guilt by association, and by ensuring proper finance and provisions to mitigate undue penalties.”<ref>《聖祖實錄·康熙九年癸巳條》</ref>
The Qing scholar {{ill|Sun Xidan|de|Sun Xidan|ja|孫希旦|zh|孫希旦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sun Xidan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} commented: “Worshiping that which is not one’s designated object—that is, worshiping ghosts that are not meant to be venerated; ‘improper’ implies transgression. For instance, when a deity not included in the canon is worshiped—as in the case of {{ill|Duke Xiang of Song|de|Herzog Xiang von Song|ja|宋襄公|zh|宋襄公|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Duke Xiang of Song|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} offering sacrifices at the secondary Shui society, or when worship exceeds one’s due share, as with the Ji Clan’s pilgrimage to [[:en:Mount Tai|Mount Tai]]—these acts are all improper worship. Was it that they had no rightful place, and yet they were exploited by foolish men and women?”<ref>《{{ill|Gazetteer of Wujiang County: Temples and Shrines|de|Wujiang-County Gazetteer: Temples and Shrines|ja|吳江縣志·祠廟|zh|吳江縣志·祠廟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gazetteer of Wujiang County: Temples and Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}》 states: “Now, in the records of temples and shrines, if one is forced to be strict and slightly relaxes the guidelines, then the unworthy will eventually exploit this precedent—selling their treachery, and when the people follow suit in their investigations, the trend will become unstoppable. In ancient times, those who rendered meritorious service to the people were enshrined in temples for a hundred generations. Was it that they had no rightful place, and yet they were exploited by foolish men and women?”</ref>

For example, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ping|de|Kaiser Ping|ja|平帝|zh|平帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ping|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平帝 (漢)}} of the {{ill|Western Han|de|Westliche Han-Dynastie|ja|西漢|zh|西漢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Western Han|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 前漢}}, there was the decree “Ban the dissemination of teachings, prohibit improper worship, and permit the sounds of Zheng.” During the {{ill|Eastern Han|de|Östliche Han-Dynastie|ja|東漢|zh|東漢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Han|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後漢}}, {{ill|Diwulun|de|Diwulun|ja|第五倫|zh|第五倫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Diwulun|qq=}} decreed “Ban and eradicate improper worship, and the people will be at peace.” At the end of the Eastern Han, [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] ordered “Ban and sever improper worship; illicit relations and the flight of offenders led to a solemnity along county boundaries.” Later, {{ill|Emperor Wen of Wei|de|Kaiser Wen von Wei|ja|魏文帝|zh|魏文帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wen of Wei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} issued an edict stating, “From now on, whoever dares to establish a rite not conforming to propriety, or whose shamanic words are uttered, shall be dealt with as following the heterodox path, as inscribed in the law.” Similarly, {{ill|Emperor Ming of Wei|de|Kaiser Ming von Wei|ja|魏明帝|zh|魏明帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ming of Wei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} decreed, “For those mountains and rivers in the counties not included in the sacrificial canon, do not venerate them.” During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Wu of Jin|de|Kaiser Wu von Jin|ja|晉武帝|zh|晉武帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wu of Jin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an edict stated “If it is not in the sacrificial canon, remove it.” Emperor Ming of the [[:en:Northern Wei|Northern Wei]] decreed “Ban improper worship, and burn all miscellaneous deities.” In the [[:en:Northern Zhou|Northern Zhou]], [[:en:Emperor Wu|Emperor Wu]] ordered “Ban all improper worship; expel anything not recorded in the liturgical canon.” In the period of the {{ill|Southern and Northern Dynasties|de|Süd- und Nord-Dynastien|ja|南北朝|lt=Southern Dynasty|lt_ja=南朝|lt_de=Süd-Dynastie|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern and Northern Dynasties|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 南北朝時代}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Wu of Song|de|Kaiser Wu von Song|ja|宋武帝|zh|宋武帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wu of Song|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 劉裕}}, it was decreed “Ban improper worship; eliminate all local temples.”
Because the definition of “improper worship” was formulated by official standards, the deities thus venerated were deemed proper, yet at the same time were regarded as lewd, demonic, and perverse spirits. Prior to the Six Dynasties, [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] in China was largely considered by scholars to be “a foreign deity not worthy of v
