revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Draft was deleted on wikipedia and preserved on everybodywiki, see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 13:36, 30 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{AfC submission|t||ts=20220918031628|u=Immanuelle|ns=118|demo=}} {{Short description|Problematic rituals in Chinese culture}} {{Expand Chinese}}
Obscene sacrifice is a term used to refer to sacrifices or [[Jesa]] which are inappropriate and contrary to national norms in the [[:en:East Asian cultural sphere|East Asian cultural sphere]], such rituals would fall under [[:en:Folk Religion|Folk Religion]]<ref>{{Cite web |title=民间信仰与官方意识 |url=http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200329052104/http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |archive-date=2020-03-29 |access-date=2020-03-29}}</ref>。
The [[:en:Book of Rites|Book of Rites]] condemns obscene sacrifice<ref>《聖祖實錄·康熙九年癸巳條》</ref>
==Overview== '''Improper Worship''' (淫祀) refers to the improper {{ill|Sacrificial Ritual|de|Opferzeremonie|ja|祭祀|zh|祭祀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacrificial Ritual|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祭}} or the worship of {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deities|de|Gottheiten|ja|神祇|lt=Divine Beings|lt_ja=神明|lt_de=Gottheiten|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Deities|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}} not included in the state‐sanctioned {{ill|Sacrificial Canon|de|Opferkanon|ja|祀典|zh|祀典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacrificial Canon|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, that is, what is nowadays known as “{{ill|Folk Religion|de|Volksreligion|ja|民間宗教|zh|民间宗教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Folk Religion|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民間信仰}}”.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |title=民间信仰与官方意识 |accessdate=2020-03-29 |archive-date=2020-03-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200329052104/http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |dead-url=no }}</ref> In ancient China, in order to maintain social order, the concept of “{{ill|Ritual|de|Ritus|ja|禮|zh|禮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritual|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” was established as the standard for upholding moral ethics, with the hope that the people, through the education of ritual, would develop sound moral values. To prevent the corruption of the populace’s ethical standards, various prohibited practices were stipulated. This idea was also found in other countries of the [[:en:Sinosphere|Sinosphere]].
With respect to religious beliefs, all forms of “improper worship” were forbidden. The “Qu Li” section of the [[:en:Book of Rites|Book of Rites]] states: “To worship that which is not rightfully one's own is called improper worship. Improper worship brings no blessing.” “By fostering filial piety and fraternal respect to uphold human relationships, by consolidating the clan to manifest harmony, by reconciling the local community to quell disputes, by emphasizing agriculture to secure sustenance, by valuing frugality to restrain extravagance, by promoting schools to cultivate proper conduct, by suppressing heterodox doctrines to elevate orthodox learning, by expounding the law to warn the foolish, by clarifying courtesy and yielding to enrich customs, by attending to one’s essential duties to stabilize the people’s resolve, by instructing the young to deter wrongdoing, by curbing false accusations to protect the virtuous, by admonishing collusion to prevent guilt by association, and by ensuring proper finance and provisions to mitigate undue penalties.”<ref>《聖祖實錄·康熙九年癸巳條》</ref>
The Qing scholar {{ill|Sun Xidan|de|Sun Xidan|ja|孫希旦|zh|孫希旦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sun Xidan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} commented: “Worshiping that which is not one’s designated object—that is, worshiping ghosts that are not meant to be venerated; ‘improper’ implies transgression. For instance, when a deity not included in the canon is worshiped—as in the case of {{ill|Duke Xiang of Song|de|Herzog Xiang von Song|ja|宋襄公|zh|宋襄公|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Duke Xiang of Song|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} offering sacrifices at the secondary Shui society, or when worship exceeds one’s due share, as with the Ji Clan’s pilgrimage to [[:en:Mount Tai|Mount Tai]]—these acts are all improper worship. Improper worship originally aimed at procuring blessings, yet it was not understood that a chaotically revered ghost cannot bestow blessings, and that a sacrifice performed in a manner contrary to ritual does not please the deities.”
== Connotations == The term “improper worship” encompasses both '''exceeding one’s due share in worship''' and '''worship not included in the sacrificial canon'''. Chinese deities are divided into three major categories: [[:en:Tenjin|Heavenly Deities]], {{ill|Earthly Deities|de|Irdische Gottheiten|ja|地祇|zh|地祇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Earthly Deities|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}}, and {{ill|Human Ghosts|de|Menschliche Geister|ja|人鬼|zh|人鬼|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Human Ghosts|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>《{{ill|Zhou Li|de|Zhou-Li|ja|周禮|zh|周禮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhou Li|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 周礼}}·Spring Officials·Grand Ancestor》: “Overseeing the rites of the heavenly deities, human ghosts, and earthly deities who establish the nation.”</ref>. Here, irrespective of whether one considers heavenly deities, earthly deities (or {{ill|Spirits of Things|de|Geister der Dinge|ja|物精|zh|物精|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirits of Things|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), the focus is solely on human ghosts. According to the sacrificial regulations recorded in the [[:en:Book of Rites|Book of Rites]]: “In the system of rites established by the sage-kings, those benefitting the people are venerated; those who die in dedicated service are honored; those who labor to secure the state are celebrated; those capable of repelling great calamities are revered; and those able to avert major misfortunes are worshiped.” Thus, apart from the ancestors venerated by every household, only individuals who have rendered meritorious service to the state or local community are entitled to be honored.
Although meritorious individuals are deserving of veneration, worshiping that which is not rightfully one’s due is also considered “improper worship”. For example, in the region of [[:en:江南|Jiangnan]], there formerly existed the “Three High Temples” which honored [[:en:Fan Li|Fan Li]], {{ill|Zhang Han (Jin Dynasty)|de|Zhang Han (Jin-Dynastie)|ja|張翰 (晉朝)|lt=Zhang Han|lt_ja=張翰|lt_de=Zhang Han|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhang Han (Jin Dynasty)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Lu Guimeng|de|Lu Guimeng|ja|陸龜蒙|zh|陸龜蒙|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lu Guimeng|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, as well as the “Three Loyal Temples” which venerated {{ill|Wu Yuan|de|Wu Yuan|ja|伍員|zh|伍員|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wu Yuan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伍子胥}}, [[:en:Zhang Xun (Tang dynasty)|Zhang Xun]], and [[:en:Yue Fei|Yue Fei]]. However, scholars generally held that the “Three High Temples” should not venerate Fan Li, because he once offered beauties and precious objects to seduce the King of Wu—thus becoming an enemy of the people of Wu: “If the rites do not honor one’s own clan, how could one possibly venerate an enemy?” Therefore, he should not be honored. Although Zhang Xun and Yue Fei were loyal, having no ties with the Wu region they should not be venerated; to do so would constitute improper worship: “In ancient times, those who rendered meritorious service to the people were enshrined in temples for a hundred generations. Was it that they had no rightful place, and yet they were exploited by foolish men and women?”<ref>《{{ill|Gazetteer of Wujiang County: Temples and Shrines|de|Wujiang-County Gazetteer: Temples and Shrines|ja|吳江縣志·祠廟|zh|吳江縣志·祠廟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gazetteer of Wujiang County: Temples and Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}》 states: “Now, in the records of temples and shrines, if one is forced to be strict and slightly relaxes the guidelines, then the unworthy will eventually exploit this precedent—selling their treachery, and when the people follow suit in their investigations, the trend will become unstoppable. In ancient times, those who rendered meritorious service to the people were enshrined in temples for a hundred generations. Was it that they had no rightful place, and yet they were exploited by foolish men and women?”</ref>
== Improper Worship in History == Improper worship—that which stands in opposition to the deities recognized by the state as orthodox—has consistently been ridiculed by scholars and, at times, forcibly suppressed. For example, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ping|de|Kaiser Ping|ja|平帝|zh|平帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ping|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平帝 (漢)}} of the {{ill|Western Han|de|Westliche Han-Dynastie|ja|西漢|zh|西漢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Western Han|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 前漢}}, there was the decree “Ban the dissemination of teachings, prohibit improper worship, and permit the sounds of Zheng.” During the {{ill|Eastern Han|de|Östliche Han-Dynastie|ja|東漢|zh|東漢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Han|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後漢}}, {{ill|Diwulun|de|Diwulun|ja|第五倫|zh|第五倫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Diwulun|qq=}} decreed “Ban and eradicate improper worship, and the people will be at peace.” At the end of the Eastern Han, [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] ordered “Ban and sever improper worship; illicit relations and the flight of offenders led to a solemnity along county boundaries.” Later, {{ill|Emperor Wen of Wei|de|Kaiser Wen von Wei|ja|魏文帝|zh|魏文帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wen of Wei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} issued an edict stating, “From now on, whoever dares to establish a rite not conforming to propriety, or whose shamanic words are uttered, shall be dealt with as following the heterodox path, as inscribed in the law.” Similarly, {{ill|Emperor Ming of Wei|de|Kaiser Ming von Wei|ja|魏明帝|zh|魏明帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ming of Wei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} decreed, “For those mountains and rivers in the counties not included in the sacrificial canon, do not venerate them.” During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Wu of Jin|de|Kaiser Wu von Jin|ja|晉武帝|zh|晉武帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wu of Jin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an edict stated “If it is not in the sacrificial canon, remove it.” Emperor Ming of the [[:en:Northern Wei|Northern Wei]] decreed “Ban improper worship, and burn all miscellaneous deities.” In the [[:en:Northern Zhou|Northern Zhou]], [[:en:Emperor Wu|Emperor Wu]] ordered “Ban all improper worship; expel anything not recorded in the liturgical canon.” In the period of the {{ill|Southern and Northern Dynasties|de|Süd- und Nord-Dynastien|ja|南北朝|lt=Southern Dynasty|lt_ja=南朝|lt_de=Süd-Dynastie|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern and Northern Dynasties|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 南北朝時代}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Wu of Song|de|Kaiser Wu von Song|ja|宋武帝|zh|宋武帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wu of Song|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 劉裕}}, it was decreed “Ban improper worship; eliminate all local temples.”
Because the definition of “improper worship” was formulated by official standards, the deities thus venerated were deemed proper, yet at the same time were regarded as lewd, demonic, and perverse spirits. Prior to the Six Dynasties, [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] in China was largely considered by scholars to be “a foreign deity not worthy of v
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Obscene temple|zh|淫祠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Obscene temple|qq=}} * [[:en:Cult|Cult]] * [[Religious persecution]] * [[:en:Folk Religion|Folk Religion]] * [[List of people who have been considered deities]]

Was it that they had no rightful place, and yet they were exploited by foolish men and women?”</ref>

For example, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ping|de|Kaiser Ping|ja|平帝|zh|平帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ping|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平帝 (漢)}} of the {{ill|Western Han|de|Westliche Han-Dynastie|ja|西漢|zh|西漢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Western Han|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 前漢}}, there was the decree “Ban the dissemination of teachings, prohibit improper worship, and permit the sounds of Zheng.” During the {{ill|Eastern Han|de|Östliche Han-Dynastie|ja|東漢|zh|東漢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Han|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後漢}}, {{ill|Diwulun|de|Diwulun|ja|第五倫|zh|第五倫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Diwulun|qq=}} decreed “Ban and eradicate improper worship, and the people will be at peace.” At the end of the Eastern Han, [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] ordered “Ban and sever improper worship; illicit relations and the flight of offenders led to a solemnity along county boundaries.” Later, {{ill|Emperor Wen of Wei|de|Kaiser Wen von Wei|ja|魏文帝|zh|魏文帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wen of Wei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} issued an edict stating, “From now on, whoever dares to establish a rite not conforming to propriety, or whose shamanic words are uttered, shall be dealt with as following the heterodox path, as inscribed in the law.” Similarly, {{ill|Emperor Ming of Wei|de|Kaiser Ming von Wei|ja|魏明帝|zh|魏明帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ming of Wei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} decreed, “For those mountains and rivers in the counties not included in the sacrificial canon, do not venerate them.” During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Wu of Jin|de|Kaiser Wu von Jin|ja|晉武帝|zh|晉武帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wu of Jin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an edict stated “If it is not in the sacrificial canon, remove it.” Emperor Ming of the [[:en:Northern Wei|Northern Wei]] decreed “Ban improper worship, and burn all miscellaneous deities.” In the [[:en:Northern Zhou|Northern Zhou]], [[:en:Emperor Wu|Emperor Wu]] ordered “Ban all improper worship; expel anything not recorded in the liturgical canon.” In the period of the {{ill|Southern and Northern Dynasties|de|Süd- und Nord-Dynastien|ja|南北朝|lt=Southern Dynasty|lt_ja=南朝|lt_de=Süd-Dynastie|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern and Northern Dynasties|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 南北朝時代}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Wu of Song|de|Kaiser Wu von Song|ja|宋武帝|zh|宋武帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wu of Song|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 劉裕}}, it was decreed “Ban improper worship; eliminate all local temples.”
Because the definition of “improper worship” was formulated by official standards, the deities thus venerated were deemed proper, yet at the same time were regarded as lewd, demonic, and perverse spirits. Prior to the Six Dynasties, [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] in China was largely considered by scholars to be “a foreign deity not worthy of v

{{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Draft was deleted on wikipedia and preserved on everybodywiki, see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 13:36, 30 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{AfC submission|t||ts=20220918031628|u=Immanuelle|ns=118|demo=}} {{Short description|Problematic rituals in Chinese culture}} {{Expand Chinese}}
Obscene sacrifice is a term used to refer to sacrifices or [[Jesa]] which are inappropriate and contrary to national norms in the [[:en:East Asian cultural sphere|East Asian cultural sphere]], such rituals would fall under [[:en:Folk Religion|Folk Religion]]<ref>{{Cite web |title=民间信仰与官方意识 |url=http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200329052104/http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |archive-date=2020-03-29 |access-date=2020-03-29}}</ref>。
The [[:en:Book of Rites|Book of Rites]] condemns obscene sacrifice<ref>《聖祖實錄·康熙九年癸巳條》</ref>
==Overview== '''Improper Worship''' (淫祀) refers to the improper {{ill|Sacrificial Ritual|de|Opferzeremonie|ja|祭祀|zh|祭祀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacrificial Ritual|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祭}} or the worship of {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deities|de|Gottheiten|ja|神祇|lt=Divine Beings|lt_ja=神明|lt_de=Gottheiten|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Deities|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}} not included in the state‐sanctioned {{ill|Sacrificial Canon|de|Opferkanon|ja|祀典|zh|祀典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacrificial Canon|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, that is, what is nowadays known as “{{ill|Folk Religion|de|Volksreligion|ja|民間宗教|zh|民间宗教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Folk Religion|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民間信仰}}”.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |title=民间信仰与官方意识 |accessdate=2020-03-29 |archive-date=2020-03-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200329052104/http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |dead-url=no }}</ref> In ancient China, in order to maintain social order, the concept of “{{ill|Ritual|de|Ritus|ja|禮|zh|禮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritual|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” was established as the standard for upholding moral ethics, with the hope that the people, through the education of ritual, would develop sound moral values. To prevent the corruption of the populace’s ethical standards, various prohibited practices were stipulated. This idea was also found in other countries of the [[:en:Sinosphere|Sinosphere]].
With respect to religious beliefs, all forms of “improper worship” were forbidden. The “Qu Li” section of the [[:en:Book of Rites|Book of Rites]] states: “To worship that which is not rightfully one's own is called improper worship. Improper worship brings no blessing.” “By fostering filial piety and fraternal respect to uphold human relationships, by consolidating the clan to manifest harmony, by reconciling the local community to quell disputes, by emphasizing agriculture to secure sustenance, by valuing frugality to restrain extravagance, by promoting schools to cultivate proper conduct, by suppressing heterodox doctrines to elevate orthodox learning, by expounding the law to warn the foolish, by clarifying courtesy and yielding to enrich customs, by attending to one’s essential duties to stabilize the people’s resolve, by instructing the young to deter wrongdoing, by curbing false accusations to protect the virtuous, by admonishing collusion to prevent guilt by association, and by ensuring proper finance and provisions to mitigate undue penalties.”<ref>《聖祖實錄·康熙九年癸巳條》</ref>
The Qing scholar {{ill|Sun Xidan|de|Sun Xidan|ja|孫希旦|zh|孫希旦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sun Xidan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} commented: “Worshiping that which is not one’s designated object—that is, worshiping ghosts that are not meant to be venerated; ‘improper’ implies transgression. For instance, when a deity not included in the canon is worshiped—as in the case of {{ill|Duke Xiang of Song|de|Herzog Xiang von Song|ja|宋襄公|zh|宋襄公|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Duke Xiang of Song|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} offering sacrifices at the secondary Shui society, or when worship exceeds one’s due share, as with the Ji Clan’s pilgrimage to [[:en:Mount Tai|Mount Tai]]—these acts are all improper worship. Improper worship originally aimed at procuring blessings, yet it was not understood that a chaotically revered ghost cannot bestow blessings, and that a sacrifice performed in a manner contrary to ritual does not please the deities.”
== Connotations == The term “improper worship” encompasses both '''exceeding one’s due share in worship''' and '''worship not included in the sacrificial canon'''. Chinese deities are divided into three major categories: [[:en:Tenjin|Heavenly Deities]], {{ill|Earthly Deities|de|Irdische Gottheiten|ja|地祇|zh|地祇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Earthly Deities|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}}, and {{ill|Human Ghosts|de|Menschliche Geister|ja|人鬼|zh|人鬼|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Human Ghosts|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>《{{ill|Zhou Li|de|Zhou-Li|ja|周禮|zh|周禮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhou Li|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 周礼}}·Spring Officials·Grand Ancestor》: “Overseeing the rites of the heavenly deities, human ghosts, and earthly deities who establish the nation.”</ref>. Here, irrespective of whether one considers heavenly deities, earthly deities (or {{ill|Spirits of Things|de|Geister der Dinge|ja|物精|zh|物精|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirits of Things|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), the focus is solely on human ghosts. According to the sacrificial regulations recorded in the [[:en:Book of Rites|Book of Rites]]: “In the system of rites established by the sage-kings, those benefitting the people are venerated; those who die in dedicated service are honored; those who labor to secure the state are celebrated; those capable of repelling great calamities are revered; and those able to avert major misfortunes are worshiped.” Thus, apart from the ancestors venerated by every household, only individuals who have rendered meritorious service to the state or local community are entitled to be honored.
Although meritorious individuals are deserving of veneration, worshiping that which is not rightfully one’s due is also considered “improper worship”. For example, in the region of [[:en:江南|Jiangnan]], there formerly existed the “Three High Temples” which honored [[:en:Fan Li|Fan Li]], {{ill|Zhang Han (Jin Dynasty)|de|Zhang Han (Jin-Dynastie)|ja|張翰 (晉朝)|lt=Zhang Han|lt_ja=張翰|lt_de=Zhang Han|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhang Han (Jin Dynasty)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Lu Guimeng|de|Lu Guimeng|ja|陸龜蒙|zh|陸龜蒙|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lu Guimeng|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, as well as the “Three Loyal Temples” which venerated {{ill|Wu Yuan|de|Wu Yuan|ja|伍員|zh|伍員|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wu Yuan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伍子胥}}, [[:en:Zhang Xun (Tang dynasty)|Zhang Xun]], and [[:en:Yue Fei|Yue Fei]]. However, scholars generally held that the “Three High Temples” should not venerate Fan Li, because he once offered beauties and precious objects to seduce the King of Wu—thus becoming an enemy of the people of Wu: “If the rites do not honor one’s own clan, how could one possibly venerate an enemy?” Therefore, he should not be honored. Although Zhang Xun and Yue Fei were loyal, having no ties with the Wu region they should not be venerated; to do so would constitute improper worship: “In ancient times, those who rendered meritorious service to the people were enshrined in temples for a hundred generations. Was it that they had no rightful place, and yet they were exploited by foolish men and women?”<ref>《{{ill|Gazetteer of Wujiang County: Temples and Shrines|de|Wujiang-County Gazetteer: Temples and Shrines|ja|吳江縣志·祠廟|zh|吳江縣志·祠廟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gazetteer of Wujiang County: Temples and Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}》 states: “Now, in the records of temples and shrines, if one is forced to be strict and slightly relaxes the guidelines, then the unworthy will eventually exploit this precedent—selling their treachery, and when the people follow suit in their investigations, the trend will become unstoppable. In ancient times, those who rendered meritorious service to the people were enshrined in temples for a hundred generations. Was it that they had no rightful place, and yet they were exploited by foolish men and women?”</ref>
== Improper Worship in History == Improper worship—that which stands in opposition to the deities recognized by the state as orthodox—has consistently been ridiculed by scholars and, at times, forcibly suppressed. For example, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ping|de|Kaiser Ping|ja|平帝|zh|平帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ping|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平帝 (漢)}} of the {{ill|Western Han|de|Westliche Han-Dynastie|ja|西漢|zh|西漢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Western Han|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 前漢}}, there was the decree “Ban the dissemination of teachings, prohibit improper worship, and permit the sounds of Zheng.” During the {{ill|Eastern Han|de|Östliche Han-Dynastie|ja|東漢|zh|東漢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Han|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後漢}}, {{ill|Diwulun|de|Diwulun|ja|第五倫|zh|第五倫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Diwulun|qq=}} decreed “Ban and eradicate improper worship, and the people will be at peace.” At the end of the Eastern Han, [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] ordered “Ban and sever improper worship; illicit relations and the flight of offenders led to a solemnity along county boundaries.” Later, {{ill|Emperor Wen of Wei|de|Kaiser Wen von Wei|ja|魏文帝|zh|魏文帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wen of Wei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} issued an edict stating, “From now on, whoever dares to establish a rite not conforming to propriety, or whose shamanic words are uttered, shall be dealt with as following the heterodox path, as inscribed in the law.” Similarly, {{ill|Emperor Ming of Wei|de|Kaiser Ming von Wei|ja|魏明帝|zh|魏明帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ming of Wei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} decreed, “For those mountains and rivers in the counties not included in the sacrificial canon, do not venerate them.” During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Wu of Jin|de|Kaiser Wu von Jin|ja|晉武帝|zh|晉武帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wu of Jin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an edict stated “If it is not in the sacrificial canon, remove it.” Emperor Ming of the [[:en:Northern Wei|Northern Wei]] decreed “Ban improper worship, and burn all miscellaneous deities.” In the [[:en:Northern Zhou|Northern Zhou]], [[:en:Emperor Wu|Emperor Wu]] ordered “Ban all improper worship; expel anything not recorded in the liturgical canon.” In the period of the {{ill|Southern and Northern Dynasties|de|Süd- und Nord-Dynastien|ja|南北朝|lt=Southern Dynasty|lt_ja=南朝|lt_de=Süd-Dynastie|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern and Northern Dynasties|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 南北朝時代}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Wu of Song|de|Kaiser Wu von Song|ja|宋武帝|zh|宋武帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wu of Song|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 劉裕}}, it was decreed “Ban improper worship; eliminate all local temples.”
Because the definition of “improper worship” was formulated by official standards, the deities thus venerated were deemed proper, yet at the same time were regarded as lewd, demonic, and perverse spirits. Prior to the Six Dynasties, [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] in China was largely considered by scholars to be “a foreign deity not worthy of v

According to the sacrificial regulations recorded in the [[:en:Book of Rites|Book of Rites]]: “In the system of rites established by the sage-kings, those benefitting the people are venerated; those who die in dedicated service are honored; those who labor to secure the state are celebrated; those capable of repelling great calamities are revered; and those able to avert major misfortunes are worshiped.” Thus, apart from the ancestors venerated by every household, only individuals who have rendered meritorious service to the state or local community are entitled to be honored.
Although meritorious individuals are deserving of veneration, worshiping that which is not rightfully one’s due is also considered “improper worship”. For example, in the region of [[:en:江南|Jiangnan]], there formerly existed the “Three High Temples” which honored [[:en:Fan Li|Fan Li]], {{ill|Zhang Han (Jin Dynasty)|de|Zhang Han (Jin-Dynastie)|ja|張翰 (晉朝)|lt=Zhang Han|lt_ja=張翰|lt_de=Zhang Han|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhang Han (Jin Dynasty)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Lu Guimeng|de|Lu Guimeng|ja|陸龜蒙|zh|陸龜蒙|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lu Guimeng|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, as well as the “Three Loyal Temples” which venerated {{ill|Wu Yuan|de|Wu Yuan|ja|伍員|zh|伍員|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wu Yuan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伍子胥}}, [[:en:Zhang Xun (Tang dynasty)|Zhang Xun]], and [[:en:Yue Fei|Yue Fei]]. However, scholars generally held that the “Three High Temples” should not venerate Fan Li, because he once offered beauties and precious objects to seduce the King of Wu—thus becoming an enemy of the people of Wu: “If the rites do not honor one’s own clan, how could one possibly venerate an enemy?” Therefore, he should not be honored. Although Zhang Xun and Yue Fei were loyal, having no ties with the Wu region they should not be venerated; to do so would constitute improper worship: “In ancient times, those who rendered meritorious service to the people were enshrined in temples for a hundred generations. Was it that they had no rightful place, and yet they were exploited by foolish men and women?”<ref>《{{ill|Gazetteer of Wujiang County: Temples and Shrines|de|Wujiang-County Gazetteer: Temples and Shrines|ja|吳江縣志·祠廟|zh|吳江縣志·祠廟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gazetteer of Wujiang County: Temples and Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}》 states: “Now, in the records of temples and shrines, if one is forced to be strict and slightly relaxes the guidelines, then the unworthy will eventually exploit this precedent—selling their treachery, and when the people follow suit in their investigations, the trend will become unstoppable. In ancient times, those who rendered meritorious service to the people were enshrined in temples for a hundred generations. Was it that they had no rightful place, and yet they were exploited by foolish men and women?”</ref>
== Improper Worship in History == Improper worship—that which stands in opposition to the deities recognized by the state as orthodox—has consistently been ridiculed by scholars and, at times, forcibly suppressed. For example, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ping|de|Kaiser Ping|ja|平帝|zh|平帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ping|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平帝 (漢)}} of the {{ill|Western Han|de|Westliche Han-Dynastie|ja|西漢|zh|西漢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Western Han|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 前漢}}, there was the decree “Ban the dissemination of teachings, prohibit improper worship, and permit the sounds of Zheng.” During the {{ill|Eastern Han|de|Östliche Han-Dynastie|ja|東漢|zh|東漢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Han|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後漢}}, {{ill|Diwulun|de|Diwulun|ja|第五倫|zh|第五倫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Diwulun|qq=}} decreed “Ban and eradicate improper worship, and the people will be at peace.” At the end of the Eastern Han, [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] ordered “Ban and sever improper worship; illicit relations and the flight of offenders led to a solemnity along county boundaries.” Later, {{ill|Emperor Wen of Wei|de|Kaiser Wen von Wei|ja|魏文帝|zh|魏文帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wen of Wei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} issued an edict stating, “From now on, whoever dares to establish a rite not conforming to propriety, or whose shamanic words are uttered, shall be dealt with as following the heterodox path, as inscribed in the law.” Similarly, {{ill|Emperor Ming of Wei|de|Kaiser Ming von Wei|ja|魏明帝|zh|魏明帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ming of Wei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} decreed, “For those mountains and rivers in the counties not included in the sacrificial canon, do not venerate them.” During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Wu of Jin|de|Kaiser Wu von Jin|ja|晉武帝|zh|晉武帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wu of Jin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an edict stated “If it is not in the sacrificial canon, remove it.” Emperor Ming of the [[:en:Northern Wei|Northern Wei]] decreed “Ban improper worship, and burn all miscellaneous deities.” In the [[:en:Northern Zhou|Northern Zhou]], [[:en:Emperor Wu|Emperor Wu]] ordered “Ban all improper worship; expel anything not recorded in the liturgical canon.” In the period of the {{ill|Southern and Northern Dynasties|de|Süd- und Nord-Dynastien|ja|南北朝|lt=Southern Dynasty|lt_ja=南朝|lt_de=Süd-Dynastie|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern and Northern Dynasties|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 南北朝時代}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Wu of Song|de|Kaiser Wu von Song|ja|宋武帝|zh|宋武帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wu of Song|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 劉裕}}, it was decreed “Ban improper worship; eliminate all local temples.”
Because the definition of “improper worship” was formulated by official standards, the deities thus venerated were deemed proper, yet at the same time were regarded as lewd, demonic, and perverse spirits. Prior to the Six Dynasties, [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] in China was largely considered by scholars to be “a foreign deity not worthy of v

revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Draft was deleted on wikipedia and preserved on everybodywiki, see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 13:36, 30 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{AfC submission|t||ts=20220918031628|u=Immanuelle|ns=118|demo=}} {{Short description|Problematic rituals in Chinese culture}} {{Expand Chinese}}
Obscene sacrifice is a term used to refer to sacrifices or [[Jesa]] which are inappropriate and contrary to national norms in the [[:en:East Asian cultural sphere|East Asian cultural sphere]], such rituals would fall under [[:en:Folk Religion|Folk Religion]]<ref>{{Cite web |title=民间信仰与官方意识 |url=http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200329052104/http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |archive-date=2020-03-29 |access-date=2020-03-29}}</ref>。
The [[:en:Book of Rites|Book of Rites]] condemns obscene sacrifice<ref>《聖祖實錄·康熙九年癸巳條》</ref>
==Overview== '''Improper Worship''' (淫祀) refers to the improper {{ill|Sacrificial Ritual|de|Opferzeremonie|ja|祭祀|zh|祭祀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacrificial Ritual|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祭}} or the worship of {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deities|de|Gottheiten|ja|神祇|lt=Divine Beings|lt_ja=神明|lt_de=Gottheiten|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Deities|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}} not included in the state‐sanctioned {{ill|Sacrificial Canon|de|Opferkanon|ja|祀典|zh|祀典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacrificial Canon|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, that is, what is nowadays known as “{{ill|Folk Religion|de|Volksreligion|ja|民間宗教|zh|民间宗教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Folk Religion|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民間信仰}}”.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |title=民间信仰与官方意识 |accessdate=2020-03-29 |archive-date=2020-03-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200329052104/http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |dead-url=no }}</ref> In ancient China, in order to maintain social order, the concept of “{{ill|Ritual|de|Ritus|ja|禮|zh|禮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritual|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” was established as the standard for upholding moral ethics, with the hope that the people, through the education of ritual, would develop sound moral values. To prevent the corruption of the populace’s ethical standards, various prohibited practices were stipulated. This idea was also found in other countries of the [[:en:Sinosphere|Sinosphere]].
With respect to religious beliefs, all forms of “improper worship” were forbidden. The “Qu Li” section of the [[:en:Book of Rites|Book of Rites]] states: “To worship that which is not rightfully one's own is called improper worship. Improper worship brings no blessing.” “By fostering filial piety and fraternal respect to uphold human relationships, by consolidating the clan to manifest harmony, by reconciling the local community to quell disputes, by emphasizing agriculture to secure sustenance, by valuing frugality to restrain extravagance, by promoting schools to cultivate proper conduct, by suppressing heterodox doctrines to elevate orthodox learning, by expounding the law to warn the foolish, by clarifying courtesy and yielding to enrich customs, by attending to one’s essential duties to stabilize the people’s resolve, by instructing the young to deter wrongdoing, by curbing false accusations to protect the virtuous, by admonishing collusion to prevent guilt by association, and by ensuring proper finance and provisions to mitigate undue penalties.”<ref>《聖祖實錄·康熙九年癸巳條》</ref>
The Qing scholar {{ill|Sun Xidan|de|Sun Xidan|ja|孫希旦|zh|孫希旦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sun Xidan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} commented: “Worshiping that which is not one’s designated object—that is, worshiping ghosts that are not meant to be venerated; ‘improper’ implies transgression. For instance, when a deity not included in the canon is worshiped—as in the case of {{ill|Duke Xiang of Song|de|Herzog Xiang von Song|ja|宋襄公|zh|宋襄公|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Duke Xiang of Song|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} offering sacrifices at the secondary Shui society, or when worship exceeds one’s due share, as with the Ji Clan’s pilgrimage to [[:en:Mount Tai|Mount Tai]]—these acts are all improper worship. Improper worship originally aimed at procuring blessings, yet it was not understood that a chaotically revered ghost cannot bestow blessings, and that a sacrifice performed in a manner contrary to ritual does not please the deities.”
== Connotations == The term “improper worship” encompasses both '''exceeding one’s due share in worship''' and '''worship not included in the sacrificial canon'''. Chinese deities are divided into three major categories: [[:en:Tenjin|Heavenly Deities]], {{ill|Earthly Deities|de|Irdische Gottheiten|ja|地祇|zh|地祇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Earthly Deities|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}}, and {{ill|Human Ghosts|de|Menschliche Geister|ja|人鬼|zh|人鬼|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Human Ghosts|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>《{{ill|Zhou Li|de|Zhou-Li|ja|周禮|zh|周禮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhou Li|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 周礼}}·Spring Officials·Grand Ancestor》: “Overseeing the rites of the heavenly deities, human ghosts, and earthly deities who establish the nation.”</ref>. Here, irrespective of whether one considers heavenly deities, earthly deities (or {{ill|Spirits of Things|de|Geister der Dinge|ja|物精|zh|物精|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirits of Things|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), the focus is solely on human ghosts. According to the sacrificial regulations recorded in the [[:en:Book of Rites|Book of Rites]]: “In the system of rites established by the sage-kings, those benefitting the people are venerated; those who die in dedicated service are honored; those who labor to secure the state are celebrated; those capable of repelling great calamities are revered; and those able to avert major misfortunes are worshiped.” Thus, apart from the ancestors venerated by every household, only individuals who have rendered meritorious service to the state or local community are entitled to be honored.
Although meritorious individuals are deserving of veneration, worshiping that which is not rightfully one’s due is also considered “improper worship”. For example, in the region of [[:en:江南|Jiangnan]], there formerly existed the “Three High Temples” which honored [[:en:Fan Li|Fan Li]], {{ill|Zhang Han (Jin Dynasty)|de|Zhang Han (Jin-Dynastie)|ja|張翰 (晉朝)|lt=Zhang Han|lt_ja=張翰|lt_de=Zhang Han|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhang Han (Jin Dynasty)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Lu Guimeng|de|Lu Guimeng|ja|陸龜蒙|zh|陸龜蒙|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lu Guimeng|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, as well as the “Three Loyal Temples” which venerated {{ill|Wu Yuan|de|Wu Yuan|ja|伍員|zh|伍員|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wu Yuan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伍子胥}}, [[:en:Zhang Xun (Tang dynasty)|Zhang Xun]], and [[:en:Yue Fei|Yue Fei]]. However, scholars generally held that the “Three High Temples” should not venerate Fan Li, because he once offered beauties and precious objects to seduce the King of Wu—thus becoming an enemy of the people of Wu: “If the rites do not honor one’s own clan, how could one possibly venerate an enemy?” Therefore, he should not be honored. Although Zhang Xun and Yue Fei were loyal, having no ties with the Wu region they should not be venerated; to do so would constitute improper worship: “In ancient times, those who rendered meritorious service to the people were enshrined in temples for a hundred generations. Was it that they had no rightful place, and yet they were exploited by foolish men and women?”<ref>《{{ill|Gazetteer of Wujiang County: Temples and Shrines|de|Wujiang-County Gazetteer: Temples and Shrines|ja|吳江縣志·祠廟|zh|吳江縣志·祠廟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gazetteer of Wujiang County: Temples and Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}》 states: “Now, in the records of temples and shrines, if one is forced to be strict and slightly relaxes the guidelines, then the unworthy will eventually exploit this precedent—selling their treachery, and when the people follow suit in their investigations, the trend will become unstoppable. In ancient times, those who rendered meritorious service to the people were enshrined in temples for a hundred generations. Was it that they had no rightful place, and yet they were exploited by foolish men and women?”</ref>
== Improper Worship in History == Improper worship—that which stands in opposition to the deities recognized by the state as orthodox—has consistently been ridiculed by scholars and, at times, forcibly suppressed. For example, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ping|de|Kaiser Ping|ja|平帝|zh|平帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ping|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平帝 (漢)}} of the {{ill|Western Han|de|Westliche Han-Dynastie|ja|西漢|zh|西漢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Western Han|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 前漢}}, there was the decree “Ban the dissemination of teachings, prohibit improper worship, and permit the sounds of Zheng.” During the {{ill|Eastern Han|de|Östliche Han-Dynastie|ja|東漢|zh|東漢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Han|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後漢}}, {{ill|Diwulun|de|Diwulun|ja|第五倫|zh|第五倫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Diwulun|qq=}} decreed “Ban and eradicate improper worship, and the people will be at peace.” At the end of the Eastern Han, [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] ordered “Ban and sever improper worship; illicit relations and the flight of offenders led to a solemnity along county boundaries.” Later, {{ill|Emperor Wen of Wei|de|Kaiser Wen von Wei|ja|魏文帝|zh|魏文帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wen of Wei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} issued an edict stating, “From now on, whoever dares to establish a rite not conforming to propriety, or whose shamanic words are uttered, shall be dealt with as following the heterodox path, as inscribed in the law.” Similarly, {{ill|Emperor Ming of Wei|de|Kaiser Ming von Wei|ja|魏明帝|zh|魏明帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ming of Wei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} decreed, “For those mountains and rivers in the counties not included in the sacrificial canon, do not venerate them.” During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Wu of Jin|de|Kaiser Wu von Jin|ja|晉武帝|zh|晉武帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wu of Jin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an edict stated “If it is not in the sacrificial canon, remove it.” Emperor Ming of the [[:en:Northern Wei|Northern Wei]] decreed “Ban improper worship, and burn all miscellaneous deities.” In the [[:en:Northern Zhou|Northern Zhou]], [[:en:Emperor Wu|Emperor Wu]] ordered “Ban all improper worship; expel anything not recorded in the liturgical canon.” In the period of the {{ill|Southern and Northern Dynasties|de|Süd- und Nord-Dynastien|ja|南北朝|lt=Southern Dynasty|lt_ja=南朝|lt_de=Süd-Dynastie|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern and Northern Dynasties|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 南北朝時代}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Wu of Song|de|Kaiser Wu von Song|ja|宋武帝|zh|宋武帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wu of Song|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 劉裕}}, it was decreed “Ban improper worship; eliminate all local temples.”
Because the definition of “improper worship” was formulated by official standards, the deities thus venerated were deemed proper, yet at the same time were regarded as lewd, demonic, and perverse spirits. Prior to the Six Dynasties, [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] in China was largely considered by scholars to be “a foreign deity not worthy of v
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also ==

The “Qu Li” section of the [[:en:Book of Rites|Book of Rites]] states: “To worship that which is not rightfully one's own is called improper worship. Improper worship brings no blessing.” “By fostering filial piety and fraternal respect to uphold human relationships, by consolidating the clan to manifest harmony, by reconciling the local community to quell disputes, by emphasizing agriculture to secure sustenance, by valuing frugality to restrain extravagance, by promoting schools to cultivate proper conduct, by suppressing heterodox doctrines to elevate orthodox learning, by expounding the law to warn the foolish, by clarifying courtesy and yielding to enrich customs, by attending to one’s essential duties to stabilize the people’s resolve, by instructing the young to deter wrongdoing, by curbing false accusations to protect the virtuous, by admonishing collusion to prevent guilt by association, and by ensuring proper finance and provisions to mitigate undue penalties.”<ref>《聖祖實錄·康熙九年癸巳條》</ref>
The Qing scholar {{ill|Sun Xidan|de|Sun Xidan|ja|孫希旦|zh|孫希旦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sun Xidan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} commented: “Worshiping that which is not one’s designated object—that is, worshiping ghosts that are not meant to be venerated; ‘improper’ implies transgression. For instance, when a deity not included in the canon is worshiped—as in the case of {{ill|Duke Xiang of Song|de|Herzog Xiang von Song|ja|宋襄公|zh|宋襄公|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Duke Xiang of Song|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} offering sacrifices at the secondary Shui society, or when worship exceeds one’s due share, as with the Ji Clan’s pilgrimage to [[:en:Mount Tai|Mount Tai]]—these acts are all improper worship. Improper worship originally aimed at procuring blessings, yet it was not understood that a chaotically revered ghost cannot bestow blessings, and that a sacrifice performed in a manner contrary to ritual does not please the deities.”
== Connotations == The term “improper worship” encompasses both '''exceeding one’s due share in worship''' and '''worship not included in the sacrificial canon'''. Chinese deities are divided into three major categories: [[:en:Tenjin|Heavenly Deities]], {{ill|Earthly Deities|de|Irdische Gottheiten|ja|地祇|zh|地祇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Earthly Deities|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}}, and {{ill|Human Ghosts|de|Menschliche Geister|ja|人鬼|zh|人鬼|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Human Ghosts|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>《{{ill|Zhou Li|de|Zhou-Li|ja|周禮|zh|周禮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhou Li|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 周礼}}·Spring Officials·Grand Ancestor》: “Overseeing the rites of the heavenly deities, human ghosts, and earthly deities who establish the nation.”</ref>. Here, irrespective of whether one considers heavenly deities, earthly deities (or {{ill|Spirits of Things|de|Geister der Dinge|ja|物精|zh|物精|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirits of Things|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), the focus is solely on human ghosts. According to the sacrificial regulations recorded in the [[:en:Book of Rites|Book of Rites]]: “In the system of rites established by the sage-kings, those benefitting the people are venerated; those who die in dedicated service are honored; those who labor to secure the state are celebrated; those capable of repelling great calamities are revered; and those able to avert major misfortunes are worshiped.” Thus, apart from the ancestors venerated by every household, only individuals who have rendered meritorious service to the state or local community are entitled to be honored.
Although meritorious individuals are deserving of veneration, worshiping that which is not rightfully one’s due is also considered “improper worship”. For example, in the region of [[:en:江南|Jiangnan]], there formerly existed the “Three High Temples” which honored [[:en:Fan Li|Fan Li]], {{ill|Zhang Han (Jin Dynasty)|de|Zhang Han (Jin-Dynastie)|ja|張翰 (晉朝)|lt=Zhang Han|lt_ja=張翰|lt_de=Zhang Han|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhang Han (Jin Dynasty)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Lu Guimeng|de|Lu Guimeng|ja|陸龜蒙|zh|陸龜蒙|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lu Guimeng|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, as well as the “Three Loyal Temples” which venerated {{ill|Wu Yuan|de|Wu Yuan|ja|伍員|zh|伍員|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wu Yuan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伍子胥}}, [[:en:Zhang Xun (Tang dynasty)|Zhang Xun]], and [[:en:Yue Fei|Yue Fei]]. However, scholars generally held that the “Three High Temples” should not venerate Fan Li, because he once offered beauties and precious objects to seduce the King of Wu—thus becoming an enemy of the people of Wu: “If the rites do not honor one’s own clan, how could one possibly venerate an enemy?” Therefore, he should not be honored. Although Zhang Xun and Yue Fei were loyal, having no ties with the Wu region they should not be venerated; to do so would constitute improper worship: “In ancient times, those who rendered meritorious service to the people were enshrined in temples for a hundred generations. Was it that they had no rightful place, and yet they were exploited by foolish men and women?”<ref>《{{ill|Gazetteer of Wujiang County: Temples and Shrines|de|Wujiang-County Gazetteer: Temples and Shrines|ja|吳江縣志·祠廟|zh|吳江縣志·祠廟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gazetteer of Wujiang County: Temples and Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}》 states: “Now, in the records of temples and shrines, if one is forced to be strict and slightly relaxes the guidelines, then the unworthy will eventually exploit this precedent—selling their treachery, and when the people follow suit in their investigations, the trend will become unstoppable. In ancient times, those who rendered meritorious service to the people were enshrined in temples for a hundred generations. Was it that they had no rightful place, and yet they were exploited by foolish men and women?”</ref>
== Improper Worship in History == Improper worship—that which stands in opposition to the deities recognized by the state as orthodox—has consistently been ridiculed by scholars and, at times, forcibly suppressed. For example, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ping|de|Kaiser Ping|ja|平帝|zh|平帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ping|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平帝 (漢)}} of the {{ill|Western Han|de|Westliche Han-Dynastie|ja|西漢|zh|西漢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Western Han|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 前漢}}, there was the decree “Ban the dissemination of teachings, prohibit improper worship, and permit the sounds of Zheng.” During the {{ill|Eastern Han|de|Östliche Han-Dynastie|ja|東漢|zh|東漢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Han|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後漢}}, {{ill|Diwulun|de|Diwulun|ja|第五倫|zh|第五倫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Diwulun|qq=}} decreed “Ban and eradicate improper worship, and the people will be at peace.” At the end of the Eastern Han, [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] ordered “Ban and sever improper worship; illicit relations and the flight of offenders led to a solemnity along county boundaries.” Later, {{ill|Emperor Wen of Wei|de|Kaiser Wen von Wei|ja|魏文帝|zh|魏文帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wen of Wei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} issued an edict stating, “From now on, whoever dares to establish a rite not conforming to propriety, or whose shamanic words are uttered, shall be dealt with as following the heterodox path, as inscribed in the law.” Similarly, {{ill|Emperor Ming of Wei|de|Kaiser Ming von Wei|ja|魏明帝|zh|魏明帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ming of Wei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} decreed, “For those mountains and rivers in the counties not included in the sacrificial canon, do not venerate them.” During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Wu of Jin|de|Kaiser Wu von Jin|ja|晉武帝|zh|晉武帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wu of Jin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an edict stated “If it is not in the sacrificial canon, remove it.” Emperor Ming of the [[:en:Northern Wei|Northern Wei]] decreed “Ban improper worship, and burn all miscellaneous deities.” In the [[:en:Northern Zhou|Northern Zhou]], [[:en:Emperor Wu|Emperor Wu]] ordered “Ban all improper worship; expel anything not recorded in the liturgical canon.” In the period of the {{ill|Southern and Northern Dynasties|de|Süd- und Nord-Dynastien|ja|南北朝|lt=Southern Dynasty|lt_ja=南朝|lt_de=Süd-Dynastie|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern and Northern Dynasties|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 南北朝時代}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Wu of Song|de|Kaiser Wu von Song|ja|宋武帝|zh|宋武帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wu of Song|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 劉裕}}, it was decreed “Ban improper worship; eliminate all local temples.”
Because the definition of “improper worship” was formulated by official standards, the deities thus venerated were deemed proper, yet at the same time were regarded as lewd, demonic, and perverse spirits. Prior to the Six Dynasties, [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] in China was largely considered by scholars to be “a foreign deity not worthy of v

revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Draft was deleted on wikipedia and preserved on everybodywiki, see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 13:36, 30 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{AfC submission|t||ts=20220918031628|u=Immanuelle|ns=118|demo=}} {{Short description|Problematic rituals in Chinese culture}} {{Expand Chinese}}
Obscene sacrifice is a term used to refer to sacrifices or [[Jesa]] which are inappropriate and contrary to national norms in the [[:en:East Asian cultural sphere|East Asian cultural sphere]], such rituals would fall under [[:en:Folk Religion|Folk Religion]]<ref>{{Cite web |title=民间信仰与官方意识 |url=http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200329052104/http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |archive-date=2020-03-29 |access-date=2020-03-29}}</ref>。
The [[:en:Book of Rites|Book of Rites]] condemns obscene sacrifice<ref>《聖祖實錄·康熙九年癸巳條》</ref>
==Overview== '''Improper Worship''' (淫祀) refers to the improper {{ill|Sacrificial Ritual|de|Opferzeremonie|ja|祭祀|zh|祭祀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacrificial Ritual|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祭}} or the worship of {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deities|de|Gottheiten|ja|神祇|lt=Divine Beings|lt_ja=神明|lt_de=Gottheiten|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Deities|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}} not included in the state‐sanctioned {{ill|Sacrificial Canon|de|Opferkanon|ja|祀典|zh|祀典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacrificial Canon|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, that is, what is nowadays known as “{{ill|Folk Religion|de|Volksreligion|ja|民間宗教|zh|民间宗教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Folk Religion|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民間信仰}}”.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |title=民间信仰与官方意识 |accessdate=2020-03-29 |archive-date=2020-03-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200329052104/http://www.doc88.com/p-6572361810409.html |dead-url=no }}</ref> In ancient China, in order to maintain social order, the concept of “{{ill|Ritual|de|Ritus|ja|禮|zh|禮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritual|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” was established as the standard for upholding moral ethics, with the hope that the people, through the education of ritual, would develop sound moral values. To prevent the corruption of the populace’s ethical standards, various prohibited practices were stipulated. This idea was also found in other countries of the [[:en:Sinosphere|Sinosphere]].
With respect to religious beliefs, all forms of “improper worship” were forbidden. The “Qu Li” section of the [[:en:Book of Rites|Book of Rites]] states: “To worship that which is not rightfully one's own is called improper worship. Improper worship brings no blessing.” “By fostering filial piety and fraternal respect to uphold human relationships, by consolidating the clan to manifest harmony, by reconciling the local community to quell disputes, by emphasizing agriculture to secure sustenance, by valuing frugality to restrain extravagance, by promoting schools to cultivate proper conduct, by suppressing heterodox doctrines to elevate orthodox learning, by expounding the law to warn the foolish, by clarifying courtesy and yielding to enrich customs, by attending to one’s essential duties to stabilize the people’s resolve, by instructing the young to deter wrongdoing, by curbing false accusations to protect the virtuous, by admonishing collusion to prevent guilt by association, and by ensuring proper finance and provisions to mitigate undue penalties.”<ref>《聖祖實錄·康熙九年癸巳條》</ref>
The Qing scholar {{ill|Sun Xidan|de|Sun Xidan|ja|孫希旦|zh|孫希旦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sun Xidan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} commented: “Worshiping that which is not one’s designated object—that is, worshiping ghosts that are not meant to be venerated; ‘improper’ implies transgression. For instance, when a deity not included in the canon is worshiped—as in the case of {{ill|Duke Xiang of Song|de|Herzog Xiang von Song|ja|宋襄公|zh|宋襄公|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Duke Xiang of Song|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} offering sacrifices at the secondary Shui society, or when worship exceeds one’s due share, as with the Ji Clan’s pilgrimage to [[:en:Mount Tai|Mount Tai]]—these acts are all improper worship. Improper worship originally aimed at procuring blessings, yet it was not understood that a chaotically revered ghost cannot bestow blessings, and that a sacrifice performed in a manner contrary to ritual does not please the deities.”
== Connotations == The term “improper worship” encompasses both '''exceeding one’s due share in worship''' and '''worship not included in the sacrificial canon'''. Chinese deities are divided into three major categories: [[:en:Tenjin|Heavenly Deities]], {{ill|Earthly Deities|de|Irdische Gottheiten|ja|地祇|zh|地祇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Earthly Deities|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}}, and {{ill|Human Ghosts|de|Menschliche Geister|ja|人鬼|zh|人鬼|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Human Ghosts|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>《{{ill|Zhou Li|de|Zhou-Li|ja|周禮|zh|周禮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhou Li|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 周礼}}·Spring Officials·Grand Ancestor》: “Overseeing the rites of the heavenly deities, human ghosts, and earthly deities who establish the nation.”</ref>. Here, irrespective of whether one considers heavenly deities, earthly deities (or {{ill|Spirits of Things|de|Geister der Dinge|ja|物精|zh|物精|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirits of Things|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), the focus is solely on human ghosts. According to the sacrificial regulations recorded in the [[:en:Book of Rites|Book of Rites]]: “In the system of rites established by the sage-kings, those benefitting the people are venerated; those who die in dedicated service are honored; those who labor to secure the state are celebrated; those capable of repelling great calamities are revered; and those able to avert major misfortunes are worshiped.” Thus, apart from the ancestors venerated by every household, only individuals who have rendered meritorious service to the state or local community are entitled to be honored.
Although meritorious individuals are deserving of veneration, worshiping that which is not rightfully one’s due is also considered “improper worship”. For example, in the region of [[:en:江南|Jiangnan]], there formerly existed the “Three High Temples” which honored [[:en:Fan Li|Fan Li]], {{ill|Zhang Han (Jin Dynasty)|de|Zhang Han (Jin-Dynastie)|ja|張翰 (晉朝)|lt=Zhang Han|lt_ja=張翰|lt_de=Zhang Han|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhang Han (Jin Dynasty)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Lu Guimeng|de|Lu Guimeng|ja|陸龜蒙|zh|陸龜蒙|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lu Guimeng|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, as well as the “Three Loyal Temples” which venerated {{ill|Wu Yuan|de|Wu Yuan|ja|伍員|zh|伍員|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wu Yuan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伍子胥}}, [[:en:Zhang Xun (Tang dynasty)|Zhang Xun]], and [[:en:Yue Fei|Yue Fei]]. However, scholars generally held that the “Three High Temples” should not venerate Fan Li, because he once offered beauties and precious objects to seduce the King of Wu—thus becoming an enemy of the people of Wu: “If the rites do not honor one’s own clan, how could one possibly venerate an enemy?” Therefore, he should not be honored. Although Zhang Xun and Yue Fei were loyal, having no ties with the Wu region they should not be venerated; to do so would constitute improper worship: “In ancient times, those who rendered meritorious service to the people were enshrined in temples for a hundred generations. Was it that they had no rightful place, and yet they were exploited by foolish men and women?”<ref>《{{ill|Gazetteer of Wujiang County: Temples and Shrines|de|Wujiang-County Gazetteer: Temples and Shrines|ja|吳江縣志·祠廟|zh|吳江縣志·祠廟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gazetteer of Wujiang County: Temples and Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}》 states: “Now, in the records of temples and shrines, if one is forced to be strict and slightly relaxes the guidelines, then the unworthy will eventually exploit this precedent—selling their treachery, and when the people follow suit in their investigations, the trend will become unstoppable. In ancient times, those who rendered meritorious service to the people were enshrined in temples for a hundred generations. Was it that they had no rightful place, and yet they were exploited by foolish men and women?”</ref>
== Improper Worship in History == Improper worship—that which stands in opposition to the deities recognized by the state as orthodox—has consistently been ridiculed by scholars and, at times, forcibly suppressed. For example, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ping|de|Kaiser Ping|ja|平帝|zh|平帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ping|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平帝 (漢)}} of the {{ill|Western Han|de|Westliche Han-Dynastie|ja|西漢|zh|西漢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Western Han|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 前漢}}, there was the decree “Ban the dissemination of teachings, prohibit improper worship, and permit the sounds of Zheng.” During the {{ill|Eastern Han|de|Östliche Han-Dynastie|ja|東漢|zh|東漢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Han|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後漢}}, {{ill|Diwulun|de|Diwulun|ja|第五倫|zh|第五倫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Diwulun|qq=}} decreed “Ban and eradicate improper worship, and the people will be at peace.” At the end of the Eastern Han, [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] ordered “Ban and sever improper worship; illicit relations and the flight of offenders led to a solemnity along county boundaries.” Later, {{ill|Emperor Wen of Wei|de|Kaiser Wen von Wei|ja|魏文帝|zh|魏文帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wen of Wei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} issued an edict stating, “From now on, whoever dares to establish a rite not conforming to propriety, or whose shamanic words are uttered, shall be dealt with as following the heterodox path, as inscribed in the law.” Similarly, {{ill|Emperor Ming of Wei|de|Kaiser Ming von Wei|ja|魏明帝|zh|魏明帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ming of Wei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} decreed, “For those mountains and rivers in the counties not included in the sacrificial canon, do not venerate them.” During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Wu of Jin|de|Kaiser Wu von Jin|ja|晉武帝|zh|晉武帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wu of Jin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an edict stated “If it is not in the sacrificial canon, remove it.” Emperor Ming of the [[:en:Northern Wei|Northern Wei]] decreed “Ban improper worship, and burn all miscellaneous deities.” In the [[:en:Northern Zhou|Northern Zhou]], [[:en:Emperor Wu|Emperor Wu]] ordered “Ban all improper worship; expel anything not recorded in the liturgical canon.” In the period of the {{ill|Southern and Northern Dynasties|de|Süd- und Nord-Dynastien|ja|南北朝|lt=Southern Dynasty|lt_ja=南朝|lt_de=Süd-Dynastie|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern and Northern Dynasties|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 南北朝時代}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Wu of Song|de|Kaiser Wu von Song|ja|宋武帝|zh|宋武帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Wu of Song|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 劉裕}}, it was decreed “Ban improper worship; eliminate all local temples.”
Because the definition of “improper worship” was formulated by official standards, the deities thus venerated were deemed proper, yet at the same time were regarded as lewd, demonic, and perverse spirits.
